sections.heading: —Application.
sections.title: Application of the Data Protection Act to personal data processing
sections.summary: This section specifies when the Data Protection Act applies to personal data processing. It covers processing entered in records by data controllers or processors using automated or non-automated means, and applies to entities established or resident in Kenya processing data within Kenya, or entities outside Kenya processing data of individuals located in Kenya.

requirements.text: Applies to processing of personal data entered in a record by or for a data controller or processor using automated or non-automated means
Applies when recorded personal data processed by non-automated means forms a whole or part of a filing system
Applies to data controllers or processors established or ordinarily resident in Kenya processing personal data within Kenya
Applies to data controllers or processors not established or ordinarily resident in Kenya but processing personal data of data subjects located in Kenya

sections.source_quote: 4. This Act applies to the processing of personal data (a) entered in a record, by or for a data controller or processor, by making use of automated or non-automated means: Provided that when the recorded personal data is processed by non-automated means, it forms a whole or part of a filing system; (b) by a data controller or data processor who — (i) is established or ordinarily resident in Kenya and processes personal data while in Kenya; or (ii) not established or ordinarily resident in Kenya, but processing personal data of data subjects located in Kenya.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines when Data Protection Act applies to personal data processing

sections.heading: —Register of data controllers and data processors.
sections.title: Public register of registered data controllers and processors
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner maintains a public register of registered data controllers and processors, which is accessible for inspection by any person. The register can be amended upon request and certified copies can be obtained, ensuring transparency and accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a public register for registered entities, which is critical for DTIAs as it allows for verification of data controller and processor registration status before international data transfers.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner shall keep and maintain a register of the registered data controllers and data processors.
The Data Commissioner may, at the request of a data controller or data processor, remove any entry in the register which has ceased to be applicable.
The register shall be a public document and available for inspection by any person.
A person may request the Data Commissioner for a certified copy of any entry in the register.

sections.source_quote: 21. (1) The Data Commissioner shall keep and maintain a register of the registered data controllers and data processors. (2) The Data Commissioner may, at the request of a data controller or data processor, remove any entry in the register which has ceased to be applicable. (3) The register shall be a public document and available for inspection by any person. (4) A person may request the Data Commissioner for a certified copy of any entry in the register.
sections.relevance_reason: Public register ensures transparency and accountability for data controllers/processors

sections.heading: —Designation of the Data Protection Officer.
sections.title: Designation of data protection officers under specific conditions
sections.summary: Data controllers or processors may appoint a data protection officer if their core activities involve systematic monitoring of data subjects, processing sensitive personal data, or operating as public bodies. The officer must avoid conflicts of interest, be accessible across entities, and possess relevant qualifications. They must publish contact details publicly and notify the Data Commissioner, and fulfill duties including compliance advice, impact assessments, and cooperation with regulators.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data protection officers for entities handling sensitive data or systematic monitoring, ensuring compliance with data protection laws and facilitating assessments for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Designate or appoint a data protection officer on terms and conditions determined by the data controller or processor
The data controller or processor must be a public body or private body (except courts in judicial capacity)
The core activities must require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects
The core activities must involve processing sensitive categories of personal data
The data protection officer may be a staff member without conflict of interest
A group of entities may appoint a single data protection officer accessible to each entity
Public bodies may designate a single data protection officer for multiple bodies considering organizational structures
The person designated must have relevant academic or professional qualifications in data protection
Publish contact details of the data protection officer on the website and communicate to the Data Commissioner
The data protection officer must advise on data processing requirements
The data protection officer must ensure compliance with this Act
The data protection officer must facilitate capacity building for staff
The data protection officer must provide advice on data protection impact assessments
The data protection officer must cooperate with the Data Commissioner on data protection matters

sections.source_quote: 24. (1) A data controller or data processor may designate or appoint a data protection officer on such terms and conditions as the data controller or data processor may determine, where — (a) the processing is carried out by a public body or private body, except for courts acting in their judicial capacity; (b) the core activities of the data controller or data processor consist of processing operations which, by virtue of their nature, their scope or their purposes, require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects; or (c) the core activities of the data controller or data processor consist of processing of sensitive categories of personal data.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection officer duties and requirements

sections.heading: —Principles of personal data protection.
sections.title: Personal data processing principles for data controllers and processors
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must process personal data lawfully, fairly, transparently, for explicit and legitimate purposes, with minimal collection, accuracy, and retention periods. Data must not be transferred outside Kenya without adequate safeguards or consent from the data subject.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data transfers outside Kenya to have adequate safeguards or explicit consent, directly impacting cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Process personal data in accordance with the right to privacy of the data subject
Process personal data lawfully, fairly, and transparently in relation to the data subject
Collect personal data for explicit, specified, and legitimate purposes and not further process in a manner incompatible with those purposes
Ensure personal data is adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary for the purposes
Collect personal data only where a valid explanation is provided for family or private affairs
Ensure personal data is accurate and kept up to date, with steps to rectify or erase inaccurate data without delay
Keep personal data in a form that identifies data subjects for no longer than necessary for the purposes
Not transfer personal data outside Kenya unless there is proof of adequate data protection safeguards or consent from the data subject

sections.source_quote: 25. Every data controller or data processor shall ensure that personal data is — (a) processed in accordance with the right to privacy of the data subject; (b) processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to any data subject; (c) collected for explicit, specified and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes; (d) adequate, relevant, limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which it is processed; (e) collected only where a valid explanation is provided whenever information relating to family or private affairs is required; (f) accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date, with every reasonable step being taken to ensure that any inaccurate personal data is erased or rectified without delay; (g) kept in a form which identifies the data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes which it was collected; and (h) not transferred outside Kenya, unless there is proof of adequate data protection safeguards or consent from the data subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection principles and cross-border transfer rules

sections.heading: —Rights of a data subject.
sections.title: Data subject rights to access, correct, delete, and object to data processing
sections.summary: Data subjects have the right to be informed about the use of their personal data, access their data, object to processing, correct false or misleading data, and request deletion of such data.
sections.dtia_summary: Directly impacts data transfer impact assessments by requiring data subjects to have control over their data, including deletion rights, which may affect data retention and transfer decisions.

requirements.text: Be informed of the use to which their personal data is to be put
Access their personal data held by the data controller or processor
Object to the processing of all or part of their personal data
Correct false or misleading data
Delete false or misleading data about them

sections.source_quote: 26. A data subject has a right — (a) to be informed of the use to which their personal data is to be put; (b) to access their personal data in custody of data controller or data processor; (c) to object to the processing of all or part of their personal data; (d) to correction of false or misleading data; and (e) to deletion of false or misleading data about them.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data subject rights

sections.heading: —Exercise of rights by data subject.
sections.title: Exercise of data subject rights by authorized representatives
sections.summary: Data subject rights may be exercised by a person with parental authority, a guardian, or a person duly authorized by the data subject, depending on the data subject's status.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it defines who can exercise data subject rights, which may affect data sharing and consent mechanisms.

requirements.text: Where the data subject is a minor, the right may be exercised by a person with parental authority or a guardian
Where the data subject has a mental or other disability, the right may be exercised by a person duly authorized as their guardian or administrator
In any other case, the right may be exercised by a person duly authorized by the data subject

sections.source_quote: 27. A right conferred on a data subject may be exercised— (a) where the data subject is a minor, by a person who has parental authority or by a guardian; (b) where the data subject has a mental or other disability, by a person duly authorised to act as their guardian or administrator; or (c) in any other case, by a person duly authorised by the data subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Data subject rights for minors/disabled

sections.heading: —Collection of personal data.
sections.title: Direct and indirect collection of personal data with lawful purposes
sections.summary: Data controllers or processors must collect personal data directly from data subjects unless specific exceptions apply, such as public records, public disclosure, consent from another source, or legal requirements for crime prevention, penalties, or protection of interests.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when indirect data collection is permissible, which affects data transfer impact assessments by allowing data from public records or other sources without direct consent.

requirements.text: Collect personal data directly from the data subject
May collect indirectly where data is in public records
May collect indirectly where the data subject has made the data public
May collect indirectly where the data subject has consented to collection from another source
May collect indirectly where the data subject has incapacity and the guardian has consented to collection from another source
May collect indirectly where collection from another source would not prejudice the data subject's interests
May collect indirectly where collection is necessary for crime prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution, punishment, or enforcement of penalties
May collect indirectly where collection is necessary for enforcement of laws imposing pecuniary penalties
May collect indirectly where collection is necessary for protection of the data subject or another person
Collect, store, or use personal data for a lawful, specific, and explicitly defined purpose

sections.source_quote: 28. (1) A data controller or data processor shall collect personal data directly from the data subject. (2) Despite sub-section (1), personal data may be collected indirectly where — (a) the data is contained in a public record; (b) the data subject has deliberately made the data public; (c) the data subject has consented to the collection from another source; (d) the data subject has an incapacity, the guardian appointed has consented to the collection from another source; (e) the collection from another source would not prejudice the interests of the data subject; (f) collection of data from another source is necessary — (i) for the prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution and punishment of crime; (ii) for the enforcement of a law which imposes a pecuniary penalty; or (iii) for the protection of the interests of the data subject or another person. (3) A data controller or data processor shall collect, store or use personal data for a purpose which is lawful, specific and explicitly defined.
sections.relevance_reason: Direct collection rules for personal data

sections.heading: —Lawful processing of personal data.
sections.title: Consent requirements for personal data processing
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must obtain explicit consent from data subjects for specific purposes or rely on lawful bases such as contractual necessity, legal obligations, vital interests, public interest tasks, public authority functions, legitimate interests, or research activities. This section establishes the conditions under which processing is lawful without consent. The requirement applies to all data processing operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit consent for cross-border data transfers where consent is the lawful basis, ensuring data transfers align with data subject consent and purpose limitation.

requirements.text: Data controller or data processor shall not process personal data without data subject consent for specified purposes

Data controller or data processor shall not process personal data without lawful basis including contractual necessity, legal obligations, vital interests, public interest tasks, public authority functions, legitimate interests, or research activities

sections.source_quote: 30. (1) A data controller or data processor shall not process personal data, unless — (a) the data subject consents to the processing for one or more specified purposes; or (b) the processing is necessary — (i) for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is a party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject before entering into a contract; (ii) for compliance with any legal obligation to which the controller is subject; (iii) in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or another natural person; (iv) for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; (v) for the performance of any task carried out by a public authority; (vi) for the exercise, by any person in the public interest, of any other functions of a public nature; (vii) for the legitimate interests pursued by the data controller or data processor by a third party to whom the data is disclosed, except if the processing is unwarranted in any particular case having regard to the harm and prejudice to the rights and freedoms or legitimate interests of the data subject; or (viii) for the purpose of historical, statistical, journalistic, literature and art or scientific research.
sections.relevance_reason: Consent requirements for lawful processing

sections.heading: —Data protection impact assessment.
sections.title: High-risk data processing requires impact assessments
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must conduct data protection impact assessments before processing personal data that poses high risk to data subjects' rights and freedoms. The assessment must detail processing operations, necessity/proportionality, risks, and risk mitigation measures. Consultation with the Data Commissioner is required if the assessment indicates high risk. Reports must be submitted 60 days prior to processing.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates data protection impact assessments for high-risk data processing, ensuring safeguards are in place before cross-border or sensitive data transfers.

requirements.text: Data controller or data processor shall carry out data protection impact assessment prior to processing if likely to result in high risk to data subject rights and freedoms
Data protection impact assessment must include systematic description of processing operations, necessity/proportionality assessment, risk assessment, and risk mitigation measures
Data controller or data processor must consult Data Commissioner before processing if impact assessment indicates high risk to data subject rights
Data impact assessment reports must be submitted sixty days prior to processing

sections.source_quote: 31. (1) Where a processing operation is likely to result in high risk to the rights and freedoms of a data subject, by virtue of its nature, scope, context and purposes, a data controller or data processor shall, prior to the processing, carry out a data protection impact assessment.
sections.relevance_reason: High-risk data processing impact assessments

sections.heading: —Processing of personal data relating to a child.
sections.title: Child data processing consent requirements
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must obtain parental or guardian consent for children's personal data processing and ensure it protects and advances the child's rights and best interests. Age verification mechanisms must be implemented based on technology, data volume, child proportion, potential harm, and Data Commissioner guidelines.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific consent mechanisms for children's data, ensuring cross-border transfers comply with child data protection standards.

requirements.text: Data controller or data processor shall not process children's personal data without parental or guardian consent
Data controller or data processor shall ensure processing protects and advances child's rights and best interests
Data controller or data processor must implement age verification and consent mechanisms
Age verification mechanisms must be determined based on available technology, data volume, child proportion, potential harm, and Data Commissioner guidelines

sections.source_quote: 33. (1) Every data controller or data processor shall not process personal data relating to a child unless — (a) consent is given by the child's parent or guardian; and (b) the processing is in such a manner that protects and advances the rights and best interests of the child.
sections.relevance_reason: Child data consent and protection core requirement

sections.heading: —Limitation to retention of personal data.
sections.title: Data retention and deletion requirements
sections.summary: Data controllers must retain personal data only as long as reasonably necessary for the purpose of processing, unless required by law, necessary for a lawful purpose, authorized by the data subject, or for historical, statistical, journalistic literature, or research purposes. Data controllers must delete, erase, anonymize, or pseudonymize data no longer necessary to retain.
sections.dtia_summary: Data retention periods impact cross-border transfers as controllers must ensure data is not retained longer than necessary, affecting data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: A data controller or data processor shall retain personal data only as long as reasonably necessary to satisfy the purpose for which it is processed
Retention may be required or authorized by law
Retention may be reasonably necessary for a lawful purpose
Retention may be authorized or consented by the data subject
Retention may be for historical, statistical, journalistic literature and research purposes
A data controller or data processor shall delete, erase, anonymise or pseudonymise personal data not necessary to be retained under sub-section (1)

sections.source_quote: A data controller or data processor shall retain personal data only as long as may be reasonably necessary to satisfy the purpose for which it is processed unless the retention is — (a) required or authorised by law; (b) reasonably necessary for a lawful purpose; (c) authorised or consented by the data subject; or (d) for historical, statistical, journalistic literature and research purposes.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data retention/deletion requirements

sections.heading: —Data protection by design or default.
sections.title: Data protection by design and default
sections.summary: Data controllers must implement technical and organizational measures to ensure data protection principles are effective and necessary safeguards are integrated into processing. Controllers must ensure only necessary data is processed by default, considering data amount, processing extent, storage period, accessibility, and processing costs. Controllers must identify risks, establish safeguards, pseudonymize or encrypt data, ensure availability, verify safeguards, and update them continuously.
sections.dtia_summary: Data protection by design requires technical and organizational measures for cross-border transfers, including pseudonymization and encryption to ensure data security during international transfers.

requirements.text: Data controllers must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to implement data protection principles effectively
Data controllers must integrate necessary safeguards into processing
Data controllers must ensure only necessary personal data is processed by default
Data controllers must consider data amount, processing extent, storage period, accessibility, and processing costs
Data controllers must identify reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to personal data
Data controllers must establish and maintain appropriate safeguards against identified risks
Data controllers must pseudonymize and encrypt personal data
Data controllers must ensure timely availability and access to personal data after a physical or technical incident
Data controllers must verify that safeguards are effectively implemented
Data controllers must ensure safeguards are continually updated in response to new risks or deficiencies

sections.source_quote: Every data controller or data processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures which are designed to implement the data protection principles in an effective manner; and to integrate necessary safeguards for that purpose into the processing.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection by design and default requirements

sections.heading: —Notification and communication of breach.
sections.title: Breach notification requirements for personal data
sections.summary: Data controllers must notify the Data Commissioner within 72 hours of unauthorized access causing real harm risk. They must communicate breach details to data subjects within a reasonable period unless identity cannot be established. Processors must notify controllers within 48 hours. Notifications must include breach nature, remedial actions, and protective measures for data subjects. Controllers may delay communication for legal investigations. Breach records must include facts, effects, and remedial actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates specific breach notification timelines and content for cross-border data transfers, critical for assessing breach response adequacy in international contexts.

requirements.text: Data controller must notify Data Commissioner within 72 hours of unauthorized access causing real harm risk
Data controller must communicate breach details to data subject within reasonable period unless identity cannot be established
Data processor must notify data controller within 48 hours of breach
Breach notification must include nature of breach, remedial actions, and protective measures for data subjects
Data controller may delay communication for legal investigations
Breach records must include facts, effects, and remedial actions

sections.source_quote: 43. (1) Where personal data has been accessed or acquired by an unauthorised person, and there is a real risk of harm to the data subject whose personal data has been subjected to the unauthorised access, a data controller shall— (a) notify the Data Commissioner without delay, within seventy-two hours of becoming aware of such breach; and (b) subject to subsection (3), communicate to the data subject in writing within a reasonably practical period, unless the identity of the data subject cannot be established.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach notification core requirement

sections.heading: —Processing of sensitive personal data.
sections.title: Sensitive personal data processing requirements
sections.summary: No sensitive personal data may be processed unless section 25 applies. This section establishes that sensitive data processing requires specific legal grounds under section 25.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets baseline for sensitive data processing, requiring specific legal grounds for cross-border transfers involving sensitive information.

requirements.text: No category of sensitive personal data may be processed unless section 25 applies

sections.source_quote: 44. No category of sensitive personal data shall be processed unless section 25 applies to that processing.
sections.relevance_reason: Sensitive data processing requires specific legal grounds

sections.heading: —Permitted grounds for processing sensitive
sections.title: Permitted grounds for sensitive personal data processing
sections.summary: Sensitive personal data may be processed for legitimate activities by non-profits with specific safeguards, where data is publicly made available by the data subject, or for legal claims, rights exercise, or vital interests protection where the data subject is incapable of consent.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines specific conditions under which sensitive data may be transferred internationally without additional safeguards.

requirements.text: Sensitive personal data may be processed by non-profits with safeguards for members only
Sensitive personal data may be processed where data is manifestly made public by data subject
Sensitive personal data may be processed for legal claims, rights exercise, or vital interests protection

sections.source_quote: 45. Without prejudice to section 44, sensitive personal data of a data subject may be processed where — (a) the processing is carried out in the course of legitimate activities with appropriate safeguards by a foundation, association or any other not-for-profit body with a political, philosophical, religious or trade union aim and on condition that — (i) the processing relates solely to the members of the body or to persons who have regular contact with it in connection with its purposes; and (ii) the personal data is not disclosed outside that body without the consent of the data subject; (b) the processing relates to personal data which is manifestly made public by the data subject; or (c) processing is necessary for— (i) the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim; (ii) the purpose of carrying out the obligations and exercising specific rights of the controller or of the data subject; or (iii) protecting the vital interests of the data subject or another person where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent.
sections.relevance_reason: Permitted grounds for sensitive data processing

sections.heading: —Personal data relating to health.
sections.title: Health data processing restrictions
sections.summary: Health data may only be processed by healthcare providers or persons under professional secrecy obligations. Processing is permitted for public health reasons or where confidentiality duties exist under law.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts health data transfers to authorized entities, critical for assessing health data cross-border compliance.

requirements.text: Health data may only be processed by healthcare providers
Health data may only be processed by persons under professional secrecy obligations
Processing is permitted for public health reasons
Processing is permitted where confidentiality duties exist under law

sections.source_quote: 46. (1) Personal data relating to the health of a data subject may only be processed — (a) by or under the responsibility of a health care provider; or (b) by a person subject to the obligation of professional secrecy under any law.
sections.relevance_reason: Health data processing restrictions directly impact data compliance

sections.heading: —Further categories of sensitive personal data.
sections.title: Sensitive data classification and safeguards
sections.summary: Data Commissioner may prescribe additional sensitive data categories and processing grounds based on risk, confidentiality expectations, potential harm, and existing protections.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows regulators to define sensitive data categories and additional safeguards for cross-border transfers involving sensitive information.

requirements.text: Data Commissioner may prescribe further categories of sensitive personal data
Data Commissioner may specify grounds for processing sensitive data based on risk, confidentiality, potential harm, and existing protections
Data Commissioner may specify additional categories requiring extra safeguards

sections.source_quote: 47. (1) The Data Commissioner may prescribe further categories of personal data which may be classified as sensitive personal data.
sections.relevance_reason: Sensitive data classification and safeguards directly impact data protection and cross-border transfers

sections.heading: — Safeguards prior to transfer of personal data out of
sections.title: Sensitive data transfers: consent and safeguards
sections.summary: Sensitive personal data transfers out of Kenya require explicit consent from the data subject and proof of appropriate safeguards. The Data Commissioner can request demonstrations of security safeguards or compelling legitimate interests. The Commissioner may prohibit, suspend, or impose conditions on transfers to protect data subjects' rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Sensitive data transfers require explicit consent and proof of adequate safeguards. The Data Commissioner has authority to restrict transfers for data protection reasons.

requirements.text: Sensitive personal data transfers out of Kenya require explicit consent from data subject
Sensitive personal data transfers out of Kenya require confirmation of appropriate safeguards
Data Commissioner may request proof of security safeguards effectiveness
Data Commissioner may request proof of compelling legitimate interests
Data Commissioner may prohibit, suspend, or impose conditions on transfers to protect data subjects' rights

sections.source_quote: The processing of sensitive personal data out of Kenya shall only be effected upon obtaining consent of a data subject and on obtaining confirmation of appropriate safeguards. The Data Commissioner may request a person who transfers data to another country to demonstrate the effectiveness of the security safeguards or the existence of compelling legitimate interests. The Data Commissioner may, in order to protect the rights and fundamental freedoms of data subjects, prohibit, suspend or subject the transfer to such conditions as may be determined.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border transfer of sensitive data requires explicit consent and safeguards

sections.heading: —Compensation of data subject.
sections.title: Compensation for data breach victims
sections.summary: A data controller or processor liable for damage caused by a breach must compensate victims. The data controller is liable for all damage, while the processor is liable only if they failed to comply with specific obligations or acted contrary to the controller's lawful instructions. Compensation includes financial loss and non-financial harm like distress.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines liability for data breaches and compensation, essential for DTIAs assessing financial and reputational impacts on data transfers.

requirements.text: A person who suffers damage by reason of a contravention of a requirement of this Act is entitled to compensation for that damage from the data controller or the data processor
A data controller involved in processing of personal data is liable for any damage caused by the processing

A data processor involved in processing of personal data is liable for damage caused by the processing only if the processor has not complied with an obligation under the Act specifically directed at data processors
A data processor involved in processing of personal data is liable for damage caused by the processing only if the processor has acted outside or contrary to the data controller's lawful instructions
A data controller or data processor is not liable in the manner specified in subsection (2) if they prove they are not responsible for the event causing damage
In this section, 'damage' includes financial loss and damage not involving financial loss, including distress

sections.source_quote: A person who suffers damage by reason of a contravention of a requirement of this Act is entitled to compensation for that damage from the data controller or the data processor.
sections.relevance_reason: Compensation for breach victims directly addresses data breach liability

sections.heading: —Annual report.
sections.title: Annual operational reports submission requirements
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner must submit an annual report within three months after each financial year ends. This report includes financial statements, activity descriptions, statistical information, impact assessments of mandates, impediments to the Act's objectives, and other necessary information related to the Office's functions.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner shall prepare and submit to the Cabinet Secretary a report of the operations of the Office for the immediately preceding year
The Cabinet Secretary shall submit the annual report before the National Assembly within three months of receipt of the report
The annual report shall contain financial statements and description of activities of the Office
The annual report shall contain statistical information as the Data Commissioner considers appropriate
The annual report shall contain impact of exercise of Data Commissioner's mandate or function
The annual report shall contain impediments to achievements of the object and purpose of this Act
The annual report shall contain any other information relating to functions that the Data Commissioner considers necessary

sections.source_quote: 70. (1) The Data Commissioner shall, within three months after the end of each financial year, prepare and submit to the Cabinet Secretary a report of the operations of the Office for the immediately preceding year.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator reporting requirements

sections.heading: —Offences of unlawful disclosure of Personal Data.
sections.title: Unlawful disclosure of personal data offences
sections.summary: Disclosing personal data in a manner incompatible with the purpose of collection constitutes an offence for data controllers without lawful excuse. Data processors who disclose data without data controller's prior authority also commit offences. Unauthorized access to personal data or disclosure to third parties without authorization is prohibited, except for employees/agents acting within their mandate.

requirements.text: A data controller who discloses personal data in any manner incompatible with the purpose for which such data was collected commits an offence
A data processor who discloses personal data without prior authority of the data controller commits an offence
A person who obtains access to personal data without prior authority of the data controller or data processor commits an offence
A person who discloses personal data to third party commits an offence
Subsection (3) does not apply to employees or agents of data controllers or processors acting within the scope of their mandate
A person who offers to sell personal data obtained in breach of subsection (1) commits an offence
An advertisement indicating personal data is or may be for sale constitutes an offer to sell the personal data

sections.source_quote: 72. (1) A data controller who, without lawful excuse, discloses personal data in any manner that is incompatible with the purpose for which such data has been collected commits an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Directly addresses unlawful data disclosure and offences

sections.heading: —Consequential amendments.
sections.title: Consequential amendments to laws for data protection compliance
sections.summary: This section amends multiple laws to incorporate data protection principles from the Data Protection Act. It requires various laws to align their processing of personal data with the Data Protection Act's principles, including financial services, employment, education, and other sectors. Specific amendments include adding new sections to ensure data protection principles apply to capital markets, electoral commissions, and other institutions. These amendments mandate that personal data be handled in accordance with data protection principles across different regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that multiple laws be amended to align with data protection principles, which may impact cross-border data transfers and processing by third parties in financial and other regulated sectors.

requirements.text: The Register shall be maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection set out in the Data Protection Act
Ensure processing of personal data in the operations of capital markets is in accordance with principles set out under the Data Protection Act

The principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply to the collection and processing of personal data by the Authority or any person authorized by the Authority
Mechanisms of protecting personal data of the data subjects in compliance with the Data Protection Act
The principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply to the processing of personal data of voters
The Commission shall ensure the management of personal data is in accordance with the principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act
Align Regulations on the collection and processing of information which consists of personal data with the Data Protection Act
Where an employer maintains such a register, the register shall be maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act
Personal data of individuals obtained under this Act shall be held and maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection set out in the Data Protection Act
The Board shall deal with any relevant personal data collected and so held in the register according to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
Any information containing personal data presented to the Commission shall be handled in accordance with data protection principles set out in the Data Protection Act
A record of depositors required by an issuer under sub-section (1) shall be issued and maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection set out in the Data Protection Act
The disclosure of information under this Act shall be done according to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
The sharing of information by the Centre shall be with adherence to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
The information collected on natural persons under this section shall be dealt according to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
Ensure necessary steps are taken to secure the integrity of personal data through technical and organizational measures
The principles of personal data protection set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply with necessary modifications to the processing and handling of bankrupt's personal data

sections.source_quote: 75. The laws specified under the Second Schedule are amended in the manner specified. The Data Protection Act 2019: CONSEQUENTIAL AMENDMENTS: Insert the following new section immediately after section 13B — 13C. The principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply to the collection and processing of personal data by the Authority or any person authorized by the Authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer compliance

sections.heading: This Act may be cited as the Data Protection Act,
sections.relevance_reason: Citation only, no data compliance content

sections.heading: 2. In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires —
