sections.heading: The object and purpose of this Act is—
sections.title: Object and purpose of the Data Protection Act
sections.summary: This section defines the overarching goals of the Data Protection Act. It mandates the regulation of personal data processing, adherence to specific principles for data processing, protection of individual privacy, establishment of legal mechanisms for data protection, and provision of data subjects with rights and remedies against improper processing.

requirements.text: Regulate the processing of personal data
Ensure processing of personal data is guided by principles in section 25
Protect the privacy of individuals
Establish legal and institutional mechanisms to protect personal data
Provide data subjects with rights and remedies to protect personal data from improper processing

sections.source_quote: 3. The object and purpose of this Act is— (a) to regulate the processing of personal data; (b) to ensure that the processing of personal data of a data subject is guided by the principles set out in section 25; (c) to protect the privacy of individuals; (d) to establish the legal and institutional mechanism to protect personal data; and (e) to provide data subjects with rights and remedies to protect their personal data from processing that is not in accordance with this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Core purpose: data protection, privacy, rights, and processing regulation

sections.heading: This Act applies to the processing of personal data
sections.title: Application of the Data Protection Act to personal data processing
sections.summary: This section specifies when the Data Protection Act applies to personal data processing. It covers processing entered in records by data controllers or processors using automated or non-automated means, and applies to entities established or resident in Kenya processing data within Kenya, or entities outside Kenya processing data of individuals located in Kenya.

requirements.text: Applies to processing of personal data entered in a record by or for a data controller or processor using automated or non-automated means
Applies when recorded personal data processed by non-automated means forms a whole or part of a filing system
Applies to data controllers or processors established or ordinarily resident in Kenya processing personal data within Kenya
Applies to data controllers or processors not established or ordinarily resident in Kenya but processing personal data of data subjects located in Kenya

sections.source_quote: 4. This Act applies to the processing of personal data (a) entered in a record, by or for a data controller or processor, by making use of automated or non-automated means: Provided that when the recorded personal data is processed by non-automated means, it forms a whole or part of a filing system; (b) by a data controller or data processor who — (i) is established or ordinarily resident in Kenya and processes personal data while in Kenya; or (ii) not established or ordinarily resident in Kenya, but processing personal data of data subjects located in Kenya.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines when Data Protection Act applies to personal data processing

sections.heading: (1) There is established the office of the Data
sections.title: Establishment of the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner
sections.summary: This section establishes the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner as a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal. It grants the Office the authority to sue and be sued, acquire and dispose of property, enter into contracts, and perform other legal acts necessary for its functions. The Office is designated as a State Office under the Constitution and includes the Data Commissioner as its head.

requirements.text: Establish the office of the Data Protection Commissioner as a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal
Be capable of suing and being sued
Take, purchase or otherwise acquire, hold, charge or dispose of movable and immovable property
Enter into contracts
Do such other legal acts necessary for proper performance of functions
Designated as a State Office in accordance with Article 260 (q) of the Constitution
Comprise the Data Commissioner as head and accounting officer, and other staff appointed by the Data Commissioner
Ensure reasonable access to services in all parts of the Republic

sections.source_quote: 5. (1) There is established the office of the Data Protection Commissioner which shall be a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal and shall in its corporate name, be capable of — (a) suing and being sued; (b) taking, purchasing or otherwise acquiring, holding, charging or disposing of movable and immovable property; (c) entering into contracts; and (d) doing such other legal acts necessary for the proper performance of the functions of the Office. (2) The Office is designated as a State Office in accordance with Article 260 (q) of the Constitution. (3) The Office shall comprise the Data Commissioner as its head and accounting officer, and other staff appointed by the Data Commissioner. (4) The Office shall ensure reasonable access to its services in all parts of the Republic. (5) The Data Commissioner shall in consultation with the Cabinet Secretary, establish such directorates as may be necessary for the better carrying of the functions of the Office.
sections.relevance_reason: State office structure for data regulator

sections.heading: (1) The Public Service Commission shall, whenever
sections.title: Appointment process for the Data Commissioner
sections.summary: This section outlines the appointment process for the Data Commissioner. The Public Service Commission initiates recruitment for vacancies, invites applications within seven days, considers applications, shortlists qualified candidates, publishes names, conducts interviews, and nominates three candidates to the President for appointment after approval by the National Assembly.

requirements.text: Public Service Commission initiates recruitment process for Data Commissioner vacancies
Public Service Commission invites applications within seven days of vacancy notification
Public Service Commission considers applications to determine compliance with the Act within twenty-one days
Public Service Commission shortlists qualified applicants
Public Service Commission publishes and publicises names of applicants and shortlisted applicants
Public Service Commission conducts interviews of shortlisted persons in an open and transparent process
Public Service Commission nominates three qualified applicants in order of merit for Data Commissioner
President nominates and appoints Data Commissioner with approval of National Assembly

sections.source_quote: 6. (1) The Public Service Commission shall, whenever a vacancy arises in the position of the Data Commissioner, initiate the recruitment process. (2) The Public Service Commission shall, within seven days of being notified of a vacancy under subsection (1), invite applications from persons who qualify for nomination and appointment for the position of the Data Commissioner. (3) The Public Service Commission shall within twenty-one days of receipt of applications under subsection (2)— (a) consider the applications received to determine their compliance with this Act; (b) shortlist qualified applicants; (c) publish and publicise the names of the applicants and the shortlisted applicants; (d) conduct interviews of the shortlisted persons in an open and transparent process; (e) nominate three qualified applicants in the order of merit for the position of Data Commissioner; and (f) submit the names of the persons nominated under paragraph (e) to the President. (4) The President shall nominate and, with approval of the National Assembly, appoint the Data Commissioner.
sections.relevance_reason: Appointment process for Data Commissioner

sections.heading: (1) A person shall be qualified for appointment as
sections.title: Qualifications for the Data Commissioner
sections.summary: This section specifies the qualifications required for appointment as Data Commissioner. Candidates must hold a degree from a recognized Kenyan university in data science, law, information technology, or another related field, have at least ten years of relevant experience, meet constitutional requirements, and hold a master's degree. The Data Commissioner serves a single six-year term without reappointment.

requirements.text: Must hold a degree from a university recognized in Kenya in data science, law, information technology, or another related field
Must have knowledge and relevant experience of not less than ten years
Must meet requirements of Chapter Six of the Constitution
Must hold a master's degree
Serves a single term of six years without reappointment

sections.source_quote: 7. (1) A person shall be qualified for appointment as the Data Commissioner if that person — (a) holds a degree from a university recognized in Kenya in — (i) data science; (ii) law; (iii) information technology; or (iv) any other related field; (b) has knowledge and relevant experience of not less than ten years; (c) meets the requirements of Chapter Six of the Constitution; and (d) holds a master's degree. (2) The Data Commissioner shall be appointed for a single term of six years and shall not be eligible for a re-appointment.
sections.relevance_reason: Qualifications for Data Commissioner

sections.heading: (1) The Office shall—
sections.title: Functions of the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner
sections.summary: This section details the functions of the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner, including overseeing implementation and enforcement of the Act, maintaining a register of data controllers and processors, exercising oversight on data processing operations, promoting self-regulation, conducting assessments, receiving and investigating complaints, taking measures to publicize the Act, carrying out inspections, promoting international cooperation, undertaking research on data processing developments, and performing other functions as prescribed by law.

requirements.text: Oversee implementation and enforcement of this Act
Establish and maintain a register of data controllers and data processors
Exercise oversight on data processing operations, either of own motion or at request of a data subject
Verify whether processing of data is done in accordance with this Act
Promote self-regulation among data controllers and data processors
Conduct assessments to ascertain whether information is processed according to provisions of this Act or other relevant laws
Receive and investigate complaints on infringements of rights under this Act
Take measures to bring provisions of this Act to knowledge of the general public
Carry out inspections of public and private entities to evaluate processing of personal data
Promote international cooperation in data protection matters and ensure compliance with international conventions and agreements
Undertake research on developments in data processing to ensure no significant risk or adverse effect on privacy of individuals
Perform such other functions as may be prescribed by any other law or necessary for promotion of Act's object

sections.source_quote: 8. (1) The Office shall— (a) oversee the implementation of and be responsible for the enforcement of this Act; (b) establish and maintain a register of data controllers and data processors; (c) exercise oversight on data processing operations, either of own motion or at the request of a data subject, and verify whether the processing of data is done in accordance with this Act; (d) promote self-regulation among data controllers and data processors; (e) conduct an assessment, on its own initiative of a public or private body, or at the request of a private or public body for the purpose of ascertaining whether information is processed according to the provisions of this Act or any other relevant law; (f) receive and investigate any complaint by any person on infringements of the rights under this Act; (g) take such measures as may be necessary to bring the provisions of this Act to the knowledge of the general public; (h) carry out inspections of public and private entities with a view to evaluating the processing of personal data; (i) promote international cooperation in matters relating to data protection and ensure country's compliance on data protection obligations under international conventions and agreements; (j) undertake research on developments in data processing of personal data and ensure that there is no significant risk or adverse effect of any developments on the privacy of individuals; and (k) perform such other functions as may be prescribed by any other law or as necessary for the promotion of object of this Act. (2) The Office of the Data Commissioner may, in the performance of its functions collaborate with the national security organs. (3) The Data Commissioner shall act independently in exercise of powers and carrying out of functions under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection enforcement functions

sections.heading: (1) The Data Commissioner shall have power to—
sections.title: Data Commissioner's investigative and enforcement powers
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner can independently investigate data breaches or complaints, seek expert advice, facilitate dispute resolution, issue witness summons, compel explanations from data subjects, impose administrative fines, and exercise other powers granted by law. These powers apply to the Data Commissioner and any person subject to the Data Protection Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the Data Commissioner's authority to investigate and enforce compliance, which is critical for conducting Data Transfer Impact Assessments as it defines the regulatory oversight mechanisms for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Conduct investigations on own initiative or based on a complaint
Obtain professional assistance or consultancy from persons or organisations
Facilitate conciliation, mediation and negotiation on disputes
Issue summons to witnesses for investigations
Require persons subject to the Act to provide explanations and information in person and in writing
Impose administrative fines for non-compliance with the Act
Undertake activities necessary for fulfilling functions of the Office
Exercise powers prescribed by other legislation

sections.source_quote: 9. (1) The Data Commissioner shall have power to— (a) conduct investigations on own initiative, or on the basis of a complaint made by a data subject or a third party; (b) obtain professional assistance, consultancy or advice from such persons or organisations whether within or outside public service as considered appropriate; (c) facilitate conciliation, mediation and negotiation on disputes arising from this Act; (d) issue summons to a witness for the purposes of investigation; (e) require any person that is subject to this Act to provide explanations, information and assistance in person and in writing; (f) impose administrative fines for failures to comply with this Act; (g) undertake any activity necessary for the fulfilment of any of the functions of the Office; and (h) exercise any powers prescribed by any other legislation.
sections.relevance_reason: enforcement powers for data breaches

sections.heading: The Data Commissioner may, subject to such
sections.title: Data Commissioner's delegation authority
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner can delegate powers under the Data Protection Act to other regulators established by Parliament, subject to conditions imposed by the Data Commissioner. This delegation is limited to powers granted by the Act or other written laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Data Commissioner to delegate enforcement powers, which may impact cross-border data transfers by enabling other regulators to oversee compliance in international contexts.

requirements.text: Delegate any power conferred under this Act or other written law to a regulator established through an Act of Parliament

sections.source_quote: 10. The Data Commissioner may, subject to such conditions as the Data Commissioner may impose, delegate any power conferred under this Act or any other written law to a regulator established through an Act of Parliament.
sections.relevance_reason: Delegation authority for regulators

sections.heading: The Office of the Data Commissioner shall
sections.title: Vacancy conditions for Data Commissioner
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner's office becomes vacant if the Commissioner dies, resigns, is convicted of an offence with imprisonment exceeding six months, is removed for reasons including inability to perform duties, constitutional non-compliance, bankruptcy, incompetence, or gross misconduct. The Public Service Commission handles removal complaints.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines scenarios where the Data Commissioner's office becomes vacant, which is relevant for DTIAs as it affects the regulatory authority overseeing data transfers.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner's office becomes vacant if the Commissioner dies
The Data Commissioner may resign from office by written notice to the President
The Data Commissioner is removed from office if convicted of an offence and sentenced to imprisonment exceeding six months without a fine option
The Data Commissioner is removed for inability to perform functions due to mental or physical infirmity
The Data Commissioner is removed for non-compliance with Chapter Six of the Constitution
The Data Commissioner is removed for bankruptcy
The Data Commissioner is removed for incompetence
The Data Commissioner is removed for gross misconduct

sections.source_quote: 11. The Office of the Data Commissioner shall become vacant, if the Data Commissioner— (a) dies; (b) resigns from office by notice in writing addressed to the President; (c) is convicted of an offence and sentenced to imprisonment for a term exceeding six months without the option of a fine; (d) is removed from office on the grounds of— (i) inability to perform the functions of office arising from mental or physical infirmity; (ii) non-compliance with Chapter Six of the Constitution; (iii) bankruptcy; (iv) incompetence; or (v) gross misconduct.
sections.relevance_reason: Vacancy conditions for Data Commissioner

sections.heading: (1) A person desiring the removal of Data
sections.title: Removal process for Data Commissioner
sections.summary: A person can remove the Data Commissioner by presenting a complaint to the Public Service Commission, which must investigate, report on facts, and recommend to the Cabinet Secretary if the complaint meets the grounds specified in section 11(d). The Data Commissioner must be informed and given a chance to defend before removal.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines the removal process for the Data Commissioner, which is relevant for DTIAs as it defines the regulatory oversight mechanisms for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: A person desiring removal may present a complaint to the Public Service Commission
The Public Service Commission must investigate the complaint expeditiously
The Public Service Commission must report on the facts
The Public Service Commission must make a recommendation to the Cabinet Secretary
The Data Commissioner must be informed in writing of reasons for intended removal
The Data Commissioner must be offered an opportunity to defend allegations

sections.source_quote: 12. (1) A person desiring the removal of Data Commissioner on any ground specified under section 11 (d) may present a complaint to the Public Service Commission setting out the alleged facts constituting that ground. (2) Subject to Article 47 of the Constitution, the Public Service Commission shall consider the complaint and, if satisfied that the complaint discloses a ground under section 11 (d), shall — (a) investigate the matter expeditiously; (b) report on the facts; and (c) make a recommendation to the Cabinet Secretary. (3) Prior to any action under sub-section (2), the Data Commissioner shall be — (a) informed, in writing, of the reasons for the intended removal; and (b) offered an opportunity to put in a defence against any such allegations.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance process for removal

sections.heading: The Data Commissioner shall in consultation with
sections.title: Staff appointment for Data Commissioner
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner must consult with the Public Service Commission to appoint the necessary staff for the Office to efficiently discharge functions under the Data Protection Act or other relevant laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures the Data Commissioner has adequate staff to oversee data transfers, which is critical for DTIAs as it affects the capacity to enforce compliance across borders.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner shall consult with the Public Service Commission to appoint staff necessary for the proper and efficient discharge of functions

sections.source_quote: 13. The Data Commissioner shall in consultation with the Public Service Commission, appoint such number of staff as may be necessary for the proper and efficient discharge of the functions under this Act or any other relevant law.
sections.relevance_reason: Staff appointment process for Data Commissioner

sections.heading: The Data Commissioner, or any staff of the Office,
sections.title: Confidentiality of data obtained
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner or any staff of the Office shall not disclose information obtained for the purposes of the Data Protection Act unless authorized by law. This ensures confidentiality of data collected during investigations or enforcement activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that data obtained during DTIAs remains confidential, which is critical for maintaining the integrity of cross-border data transfers and preventing unauthorized disclosures.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner or any staff of the Office shall not disclose any information obtained for the purposes of this Act unless with lawful authority

sections.source_quote: 16. The Data Commissioner, or any staff of the Office, shall not, unless with lawful authority, disclose any information obtained for the purposes of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Confidentiality of data obtained

sections.heading: The Data Coir‘missioner or any staff of the Office
sections.title: Liability exemption for Data Commissioner and staff
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner and their staff are exempt from liability for performing functions in good faith and in accordance with the Data Protection Act. This provision ensures that officials are protected from legal consequences when acting within their statutory duties as defined by the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes that Data Commissioner and staff are not liable for lawful actions, which is relevant for DTIAs as it clarifies the regulatory authority's accountability without imposing additional cross-border transfer constraints.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner or any staff of the Office shall not be held liable for having performed any of their functions in good faith and in accordance with this Act.

sections.source_quote: 17. The Data Commissioner or any staff of the Office shall not be held liable for having performed any of their functions in good faith and in accordance with this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Liability exemption for Data Commissioner

sections.heading: (1) Subject to sub-section (2), no person shall act
sections.title: Mandatory registration for data controllers and processors
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must register with the Data Commissioner unless exempted. Registration thresholds are determined by the Data Commissioner based on industry type, data volumes, sensitive data processing, and other criteria. This ensures that entities handling personal data are properly vetted before commencing operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandatory registration with defined thresholds affects DTIAs by requiring entities to assess and meet specific criteria before transferring data internationally, ensuring compliance with local data governance.

requirements.text: No person shall act as a data controller or data processor unless registered with the Data Commissioner.
The Data Commissioner shall prescribe thresholds required for mandatory registration of data controllers and data processors.
The Data Commissioner shall consider the nature of industry, volumes of data processed, whether sensitive personal data is being processed, and any other criteria when prescribing thresholds.

sections.source_quote: 18. (1) Subject to sub-section (2), no person shall act as a data controller or data processor unless registered with the Data Commissioner. (2) The Data Commissioner shall prescribe thresholds required for mandatory registration of data controllers and data processors, and in making such determination, the Data Commissioner shall consider — (a) the nature of industry; (b) the volumes of data processed; (c) whether sensitive personal data is being processed; and (d) any other criteria the Data Commissioner may specify.
sections.relevance_reason: Mandatory registration for data controllers/processors

sections.heading: (1) A data controller or data processor required to
sections.title: Application and registration process for data controllers and processors
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must submit detailed applications to the Data Commissioner, including descriptions of data processed, purposes, data subjects, contact details, risk assessments, security measures, and indemnification mechanisms. Applications must be truthful; false information constitutes an offence. The Data Commissioner issues certificates upon meeting requirements and requires updates for changes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed registration applications, which are critical for DTIAs as they require thorough assessment of data handling practices before international transfers, including security and risk management.

requirements.text: A data controller or data processor required to register shall apply to the Data Commissioner.
An application shall provide a description of the personal data to be processed.
An application shall provide a description of the purpose for which the personal data is to be processed.
An application shall provide the category of data subjects to which the personal data relates.
An application shall provide contact details of the data controller or data processor.
An application shall provide a general description of the risks, safeguards, security measures, and mechanisms to ensure the protection of personal data.
An application shall provide any measures to indemnify the data subject from unlawful use of data by the data processor or data controller.
An application shall provide any other details as may be prescribed by the Data Commissioner.
A data controller or data processor who knowingly supplies false or misleading detail commits an offence.
The Data Commissioner shall issue a certificate of registration where a data controller or data processor meets the requirements for registration.
A data controller or data processor shall notify the Data Commissioner of a change in any particular outlined under subsection (2).
On receipt of a notification under sub-section (5), the Data Commissioner shall amend the respective entry in the Register.
A data controller or data processor who fails to comply with the provisions of this section commits an offence.

sections.source_quote: 19. (1) A data controller or data processor required to register under section 18 shall apply to the Data Commissioner. (2) An application under sub-section (1) shall provide the following particulars — (a) a description of the personal data to be processed by the data controller or data processor; (b) a description of the purpose for which the personal data is to be processed; (c) the category of data subjects, to which the personal data relates; (d) contact details of the data controller or data processor; (e) a general description of the risks, safeguards, security measures and mechanisms to ensure the protection of personal data; (f) any measures to indemnify the data subject from unlawful use of data by the data processor or data controller; and (g) any other details as may be prescribed by the Data Commissioner.
sections.relevance_reason: Core registration process for data controllers/processors

sections.heading: A registration certificate issued under section 19
sections.title: Validity and renewal of registration certificates
sections.summary: Registration certificates issued under section 19 are valid for a period determined by the Data Commissioner, considering the need for the certificate and the holder's circumstances. The certificate may be renewed after expiry, ensuring ongoing compliance without permanent registration.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that registration certificates have defined validity periods, which is relevant for DTIAs as it requires entities to maintain valid registration status for international data transfers.

requirements.text: A registration certificate issued under section 19 shall be valid for a period determined at the time of the application after taking into account the need for the certificate.
The holder may apply for a renewal of the certificate after expiry of the certificate.

sections.source_quote: 20. A registration certificate issued under section 19 shall be valid for a period determined at the time of the application after taking into account the need for the certificate, and the holder may apply for a renewal of the certificate after expiry of the certificate.
sections.relevance_reason: certificate renewal for ongoing compliance

sections.heading: (1) The Data Commissioner shall keep and
sections.title: Public register of registered data controllers and processors
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner maintains a public register of registered data controllers and processors, which is accessible for inspection by any person. The register can be amended upon request and certified copies can be obtained, ensuring transparency and accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a public register for registered entities, which is critical for DTIAs as it allows for verification of data controller and processor registration status before international data transfers.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner shall keep and maintain a register of the registered data controllers and data processors.
The Data Commissioner may, at the request of a data controller or data processor, remove any entry in the register which has ceased to be applicable.
The register shall be a public document and available for inspection by any person.
A person may request the Data Commissioner for a certified copy of any entry in the register.

sections.source_quote: 21. (1) The Data Commissioner shall keep and maintain a register of the registered data controllers and data processors. (2) The Data Commissioner may, at the request of a data controller or data processor, remove any entry in the register which has ceased to be applicable. (3) The register shall be a public document and available for inspection by any person. (4) A person may request the Data Commissioner for a certified copy of any entry in the register.
sections.relevance_reason: Public register ensures transparency and accountability for data controllers/processors

sections.heading: The Data Commissioner may, on issuance of a
sections.title: Cancellation or variation of registration certificates
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may cancel or vary registration certificates if an applicant provides false information or fails to comply with the Act's requirements. This ensures that only compliant entities maintain registration status.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for the cancellation of registration certificates based on non-compliance, which is relevant for DTIAs as it requires entities to maintain ongoing compliance with data protection standards before international transfers.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may, on issuance of a notice to show cause, vary terms and conditions of the certificate of registration or cancel the registration where any information given by the applicant is false or misleading.

The Data Commissioner may, on issuance of a notice to show cause, vary terms and conditions of the certificate of registration or cancel the registration where the holder of the registration certificate, without lawful excuse, fails to comply with any requirement of this Act.

sections.source_quote: 22. The Data Commissioner may, on issuance of a notice to show cause, vary terms and conditions of the certificate of registration or cancel the registration where — (a) any information given by the applicant is false or misleading; or (b) the holder of the registration certificate, without lawful excuse, fails to comply with any requirement of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for registration compliance

sections.heading: (1) A data controller or data processor may
sections.title: Designation of data protection officers under specific conditions
sections.summary: Data controllers or processors may appoint a data protection officer if their core activities involve systematic monitoring of data subjects, processing sensitive personal data, or operating as public bodies. The officer must avoid conflicts of interest, be accessible across entities, and possess relevant qualifications. They must publish contact details publicly and notify the Data Commissioner, and fulfill duties including compliance advice, impact assessments, and cooperation with regulators.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data protection officers for entities handling sensitive data or systematic monitoring, ensuring compliance with data protection laws and facilitating assessments for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Designate or appoint a data protection officer on terms and conditions determined by the data controller or processor
The data controller or processor must be a public body or private body (except courts in judicial capacity)
The core activities must require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects
The core activities must involve processing sensitive categories of personal data
The data protection officer may be a staff member without conflict of interest
A group of entities may appoint a single data protection officer accessible to each entity
Public bodies may designate a single data protection officer for multiple bodies considering organizational structures
The person designated must have relevant academic or professional qualifications in data protection
Publish contact details of the data protection officer on the website and communicate to the Data Commissioner
The data protection officer must advise on data processing requirements
The data protection officer must ensure compliance with this Act
The data protection officer must facilitate capacity building for staff
The data protection officer must provide advice on data protection impact assessments
The data protection officer must cooperate with the Data Commissioner on data protection matters

sections.source_quote: 24. (1) A data controller or data processor may designate or appoint a data protection officer on such terms and conditions as the data controller or data processor may determine, where — (a) the processing is carried out by a public body or private body, except for courts acting in their judicial capacity; (b) the core activities of the data controller or data processor consist of processing operations which, by virtue of their nature, their scope or their purposes, require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects; or (c) the core activities of the data controller or data processor consist of processing of sensitive categories of personal data.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection officer duties and requirements

sections.heading: Every data controller or data processor shall
sections.title: Personal data processing principles for data controllers and processors
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must process personal data lawfully, fairly, transparently, for explicit and legitimate purposes, with minimal collection, accuracy, and retention periods. Data must not be transferred outside Kenya without adequate safeguards or consent from the data subject.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data transfers outside Kenya to have adequate safeguards or explicit consent, directly impacting cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Process personal data in accordance with the right to privacy of the data subject
Process personal data lawfully, fairly, and transparently in relation to the data subject
Collect personal data for explicit, specified, and legitimate purposes and not further process in a manner incompatible with those purposes
Ensure personal data is adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary for the purposes
Collect personal data only where a valid explanation is provided for family or private affairs
Ensure personal data is accurate and kept up to date, with steps to rectify or erase inaccurate data without delay
Keep personal data in a form that identifies data subjects for no longer than necessary for the purposes
Not transfer personal data outside Kenya unless there is proof of adequate data protection safeguards or consent from the data subject

sections.source_quote: 25. Every data controller or data processor shall ensure that personal data is — (a) processed in accordance with the right to privacy of the data subject; (b) processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to any data subject; (c) collected for explicit, specified and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes; (d) adequate, relevant, limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which it is processed; (e) collected only where a valid explanation is provided whenever information relating to family or private affairs is required; (f) accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date, with every reasonable step being taken to ensure that any inaccurate personal data is erased or rectified without delay; (g) kept in a form which identifies the data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes which it was collected; and (h) not transferred outside Kenya, unless there is proof of adequate data protection safeguards or consent from the data subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection principles and cross-border transfer rules

sections.heading: A data subject has a right —
sections.title: Data subject rights to access, correct, delete, and object to data processing
sections.summary: Data subjects have the right to be informed about the use of their personal data, access their data, object to processing, correct false or misleading data, and request deletion of such data.
sections.dtia_summary: Directly impacts data transfer impact assessments by requiring data subjects to have control over their data, including deletion rights, which may affect data retention and transfer decisions.

requirements.text: Be informed of the use to which their personal data is to be put
Access their personal data held by the data controller or processor
Object to the processing of all or part of their personal data
Correct false or misleading data
Delete false or misleading data about them

sections.source_quote: 26. A data subject has a right — (a) to be informed of the use to which their personal data is to be put; (b) to access their personal data in custody of data controller or data processor; (c) to object to the processing of all or part of their personal data; (d) to correction of false or misleading data; and (e) to deletion of false or misleading data about them.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data subject rights

sections.heading: A right conferred on a data subject may be
sections.title: Exercise of data subject rights by authorized representatives
sections.summary: Data subject rights may be exercised by a person with parental authority, a guardian, or a person duly authorized by the data subject, depending on the data subject's status.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it defines who can exercise data subject rights, which may affect data sharing and consent mechanisms.

requirements.text: Where the data subject is a minor, the right may be exercised by a person with parental authority or a guardian
Where the data subject has a mental or other disability, the right may be exercised by a person duly authorized as their guardian or administrator
In any other case, the right may be exercised by a person duly authorized by the data subject

sections.source_quote: 27. A right conferred on a data subject may be exercised— (a) where the data subject is a minor, by a person who has parental authority or by a guardian; (b) where the data subject has a mental or other disability, by a person duly authorised to act as their guardian or administrator; or (c) in any other case, by a person duly authorised by the data subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Data subject rights for minors/disabled

sections.heading: (1) A data controller or data processor shall collect
sections.title: Direct and indirect collection of personal data with lawful purposes
sections.summary: Data controllers or processors must collect personal data directly from data subjects unless specific exceptions apply, such as public records, public disclosure, consent from another source, or legal requirements for crime prevention, penalties, or protection of interests.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when indirect data collection is permissible, which affects data transfer impact assessments by allowing data from public records or other sources without direct consent.

requirements.text: Collect personal data directly from the data subject
May collect indirectly where data is in public records
May collect indirectly where the data subject has made the data public
May collect indirectly where the data subject has consented to collection from another source
May collect indirectly where the data subject has incapacity and the guardian has consented to collection from another source
May collect indirectly where collection from another source would not prejudice the data subject's interests
May collect indirectly where collection is necessary for crime prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution, punishment, or enforcement of penalties
May collect indirectly where collection is necessary for enforcement of laws imposing pecuniary penalties
May collect indirectly where collection is necessary for protection of the data subject or another person
Collect, store, or use personal data for a lawful, specific, and explicitly defined purpose

sections.source_quote: 28. (1) A data controller or data processor shall collect personal data directly from the data subject. (2) Despite sub-section (1), personal data may be collected indirectly where — (a) the data is contained in a public record; (b) the data subject has deliberately made the data public; (c) the data subject has consented to the collection from another source; (d) the data subject has an incapacity, the guardian appointed has consented to the collection from another source; (e) the collection from another source would not prejudice the interests of the data subject; (f) collection of data from another source is necessary — (i) for the prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution and punishment of crime; (ii) for the enforcement of a law which imposes a pecuniary penalty; or (iii) for the protection of the interests of the data subject or another person. (3) A data controller or data processor shall collect, store or use personal data for a purpose which is lawful, specific and explicitly defined.
sections.relevance_reason: Direct collection rules for personal data

sections.heading: A data controller or data processor shall, before
sections.title: Informed consent and disclosure requirements before data collection
sections.summary: Before collecting personal data, data controllers or processors must inform data subjects of their rights, the purpose of collection, third parties receiving data, security measures, legal basis, and consequences of non-compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed disclosure of data transfer mechanisms and security measures, which is critical for assessing cross-border data transfers and ensuring compliance with international standards.

requirements.text: Inform the data subject of the rights specified under section 26
Inform the data subject that personal data is being collected
Inform the data subject of the purpose for which personal data is being collected
Inform the data subject of third parties whose personal data has been or will be transferred, including details of safeguards
Inform the data subject of the contacts of the data controller or processor and whether other entities may receive the data
Inform the data subject of technical and organizational security measures for data integrity and confidentiality
Inform the data subject of whether the data collection is pursuant to any law and whether it is voluntary or mandatory
Inform the data subject of consequences if they fail to provide all or any part of requested data

sections.source_quote: 29. A data controller or data processor shall, before collecting personal data, in so far as practicable, inform the data subject of — (a) the rights of data subject specified under section 26; (b) the fact that personal data is being collected; (c) the purpose for which the personal data is being collected; (d) the third parties whose personal data has been or will be transferred to, including details of safeguards adopted; (e) the contacts of the data controller or data processor and on whether any other entity may receive the collected personal data; (f) a description of the technical and organizational security measures taken to ensure the integrity and confidentiality of the data; (g) the data being collected pursuant to any law and whether such collection is voluntary or mandatory; and (h) the consequences if any, where the data subject fails to provide all or any part of the requested data.
sections.relevance_reason: Core consent and disclosure requirements for data collection

sections.heading: (1) A data controller or data processor shall not
sections.title: Consent requirements for personal data processing
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must obtain explicit consent from data subjects for specific purposes or rely on lawful bases such as contractual necessity, legal obligations, vital interests, public interest tasks, public authority functions, legitimate interests, or research activities. This section establishes the conditions under which processing is lawful without consent. The requirement applies to all data processing operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit consent for cross-border data transfers where consent is the lawful basis, ensuring data transfers align with data subject consent and purpose limitation.

requirements.text: Data controller or data processor shall not process personal data without data subject consent for specified purposes

Data controller or data processor shall not process personal data without lawful basis including contractual necessity, legal obligations, vital interests, public interest tasks, public authority functions, legitimate interests, or research activities

sections.source_quote: 30. (1) A data controller or data processor shall not process personal data, unless — (a) the data subject consents to the processing for one or more specified purposes; or (b) the processing is necessary — (i) for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is a party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject before entering into a contract; (ii) for compliance with any legal obligation to which the controller is subject; (iii) in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or another natural person; (iv) for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; (v) for the performance of any task carried out by a public authority; (vi) for the exercise, by any person in the public interest, of any other functions of a public nature; (vii) for the legitimate interests pursued by the data controller or data processor by a third party to whom the data is disclosed, except if the processing is unwarranted in any particular case having regard to the harm and prejudice to the rights and freedoms or legitimate interests of the data subject; or (viii) for the purpose of historical, statistical, journalistic, literature and art or scientific research.
sections.relevance_reason: Consent requirements for lawful processing

sections.heading: (1) Where a processing operation is likely to result
sections.title: High-risk data processing requires impact assessments
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must conduct data protection impact assessments before processing personal data that poses high risk to data subjects' rights and freedoms. The assessment must detail processing operations, necessity/proportionality, risks, and risk mitigation measures. Consultation with the Data Commissioner is required if the assessment indicates high risk. Reports must be submitted 60 days prior to processing.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates data protection impact assessments for high-risk data processing, ensuring safeguards are in place before cross-border or sensitive data transfers.

requirements.text: Data controller or data processor shall carry out data protection impact assessment prior to processing if likely to result in high risk to data subject rights and freedoms
Data protection impact assessment must include systematic description of processing operations, necessity/proportionality assessment, risk assessment, and risk mitigation measures
Data controller or data processor must consult Data Commissioner before processing if impact assessment indicates high risk to data subject rights
Data impact assessment reports must be submitted sixty days prior to processing

sections.source_quote: 31. (1) Where a processing operation is likely to result in high risk to the rights and freedoms of a data subject, by virtue of its nature, scope, context and purposes, a data controller or data processor shall, prior to the processing, carry out a data protection impact assessment.
sections.relevance_reason: High-risk data processing impact assessments

sections.heading: (1) A data controller or data processor shall bear
sections.title: Consent burden and withdrawal rights
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must prove data subject consent for specific purposes. Data subjects can withdraw consent at any time without affecting prior lawful processing. Consent must be freely given, considering whether contractual performance is conditional on consent for non-essential data processing.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines consent requirements for cross-border data transfers, ensuring data subjects retain control over their data and can withdraw consent without invalidating prior lawful transfers.

requirements.text: Data controller or data processor shall bear burden of proof for establishing data subject consent
Data subject may withdraw consent at any time unless otherwise provided
Withdrawal of consent does not affect lawfulness of prior processing
Consent must be freely given, considering whether contractual performance is conditional on consent for non-essential data processing

sections.source_quote: 32. (1) A data controller or data processor shall bear the burden of proof for establishing a data subject's consent to the processing of their personal data for a specified purpose.
sections.relevance_reason: Consent burden and withdrawal rights directly impact data compliance

sections.heading: (1) Every c.L.,ta controller or data processor shall
sections.title: Child data processing consent requirements
sections.summary: Data controllers and processors must obtain parental or guardian consent for children's personal data processing and ensure it protects and advances the child's rights and best interests. Age verification mechanisms must be implemented based on technology, data volume, child proportion, potential harm, and Data Commissioner guidelines.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific consent mechanisms for children's data, ensuring cross-border transfers comply with child data protection standards.

requirements.text: Data controller or data processor shall not process children's personal data without parental or guardian consent
Data controller or data processor shall ensure processing protects and advances child's rights and best interests
Data controller or data processor must implement age verification and consent mechanisms
Age verification mechanisms must be determined based on available technology, data volume, child proportion, potential harm, and Data Commissioner guidelines

sections.source_quote: 33. (1) Every data controller or data processor shall not process personal data relating to a child unless — (a) consent is given by the child's parent or guardian; and (b) the processing is in such a manner that protects and advances the rights and best interests of the child.
sections.relevance_reason: Child data consent and protection core requirement

sections.heading: (1) A data controller or data processor shall, at the
sections.title: Data subject right to restrict processing
sections.summary: Data subjects can restrict processing of their personal data if accuracy is contested, data is no longer needed, processing is unlawful, or they object pending verification of legitimate interests. Restricted data may only be processed with consent, legal claims, protection of others' rights, or public interest. Data controllers must implement mechanisms to ensure time limits for restrictions are observed.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows data subjects to restrict data processing across borders, ensuring data transfers comply with data subject rights and time-bound restrictions.

requirements.text: Data subject may restrict processing if accuracy is contested for verification period
Data subject may restrict processing if data is no longer required for processing purposes
Data subject may restrict processing if processing is unlawful and they oppose erasure
Data subject may restrict processing if they object pending verification of legitimate interests
Restricted data may only be processed with consent, legal claims, protection of others' rights, or public interest
Data controller must inform data subject before withdrawing restriction on processing
Data controller must implement mechanisms to ensure time limits for restrictions are observed

sections.source_quote: 34. (1) A data controller or data processor shall, at the request of a data subject, restrict the processing of personal data where— (a) accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the data controller to verify the accuracy of the data; (b) personal data is no longer required for the purpose of the processing, unless the data controller or data processor requires the personal data for the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim; (c) processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure of the personal data and requests the restriction of their use instead; or (d) data subject has objected to the processing, pending verification as to whether the legitimate interests of the data controller or data processor overrides those of the data subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Data subject right to restrict processing

sections.heading: (1) Every data subject has a right not to be subject
sections.title: Right to opt out of automated decisions
sections.summary: Data subjects have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal effects or significantly affect them. Exceptions include contractual necessity, legally authorized measures, and consent. Data controllers must notify data subjects of automated decisions and allow them to request reconsideration or alternative decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires transparency and opt-out mechanisms for automated decisions, ensuring cross-border data transfers do not violate data subject rights through automated profiling.

requirements.text: Data subject has right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal effects or significantly affect them
Automated decisions may be exempt if necessary for contracts, authorized by law, or based on consent
Data controller must notify data subject in writing of automated decision within reasonable time
Data subject may request reconsideration or alternative decision after notification
Data controller must consider request and provide written notice of actions taken

sections.source_quote: 35. (1) Every data subject has a right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning or significantly affects the data subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Right to opt out of automated decisions

sections.heading: A data subject has a right to object to the
sections.title: Right to object data processing with exceptions
sections.summary: A data subject can object to the processing of their personal data. The data controller or processor must demonstrate compelling legitimate interest that overrides the data subject's interests or is necessary for legal claims. This right applies to all data controllers and processors handling personal data.
sections.dtia_summary: Data transfer requires demonstrating compelling legitimate interest to override data subject objections. This impacts cross-border transfers where data subject rights must be respected.

requirements.text: A data subject has the right to object to the processing of their personal data
The data controller or data processor must demonstrate compelling legitimate interest that overrides the data subject's interests
The data controller or data processor must demonstrate compelling legitimate interest for the processing which is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim

sections.source_quote: A data subject has a right to object to the processing of their personal data, unless the data controller or data processor demonstrates compelling legitimate interest for the processing which overrides the data subject's interests, or for the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim.
sections.relevance_reason: Right to object data processing

sections.heading: (1) A person shall not use, for commercial
sections.title: Commercial use of personal data restrictions
sections.summary: Personal data used for commercial purposes must be obtained with express consent from the data subject or authorized under written law with prior notification. Data controllers must anonymize data to ensure non-identifiability where possible. The Cabinet Secretary may prescribe practice guidelines for commercial use of personal data.
sections.dtia_summary: Commercial data use requires consent or legal authorization with anonymization for cross-border transfers to ensure data subject rights are protected.

requirements.text: A person shall not use personal data for commercial purposes unless the person has sought and obtained express consent from a data subject
A person shall not use personal data for commercial purposes unless the person is authorized under written law and the data subject has been informed of such use
A data controller or data processor shall anonymize personal data in a manner ensuring the data subject is no longer identifiable
The Cabinet Secretary may prescribe practice guidelines for commercial use of personal data

sections.source_quote: (1) A person shall not use, for commercial purposes, personal data obtained pursuant to the provisions of this Act unless the person — (a) has sought and obtained express consent from a data subject; or (b) is authorised to do so under any written law and the data subject has been informed of such use when collecting the data from the data subject.
sections.relevance_reason: Commercial use restrictions on personal data

sections.heading: (1) A data subject has the right to receive personal
sections.title: Right to data portability and transfer
sections.summary: Data subjects have the right to receive personal data in a structured, machine-readable format and transmit it to another controller or processor without hindrance. Technical direct transfer is preferred where possible. Data controllers must comply with portability requests within thirty days at reasonable cost, with extensions for complex requests. The right does not apply for public interest tasks or when rights of others are affected.
sections.dtia_summary: Data portability rights require controllers to provide data in machine-readable format and facilitate transfer, impacting cross-border data transfers where data subjects may request transfers.

requirements.text: A data subject has the right to receive personal data concerning them in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format
A data subject has the right to transmit the data obtained to another data controller or data processor without any hindrance
Where technically possible, the data subject shall have the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one data controller or processor to another
The Data Commissioner may make a determination on the technical capacity of the data controller or data processor if they decline to comply with sub-section (3)
A data controller or data processor shall comply with data portability requests at reasonable cost and within thirty days
Where portability request is complex or numerous, the period may be extended for a further period as determined with the Data Commissioner

sections.source_quote: A data subject has the right to receive personal data concerning them in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format. A data subject has the right to transmit the data obtained under sub-section (1), to another data controller or data processor without any hindrance.
sections.relevance_reason: Data portability rights directly impact cross-border data transfers and data subject rights

sections.heading: (1) A data controller or data processor shall retain
sections.title: Data retention and deletion requirements
sections.summary: Data controllers must retain personal data only as long as reasonably necessary for the purpose of processing, unless required by law, necessary for a lawful purpose, authorized by the data subject, or for historical, statistical, journalistic literature, or research purposes. Data controllers must delete, erase, anonymize, or pseudonymize data no longer necessary to retain.
sections.dtia_summary: Data retention periods impact cross-border transfers as controllers must ensure data is not retained longer than necessary, affecting data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: A data controller or data processor shall retain personal data only as long as reasonably necessary to satisfy the purpose for which it is processed
Retention may be required or authorized by law
Retention may be reasonably necessary for a lawful purpose
Retention may be authorized or consented by the data subject
Retention may be for historical, statistical, journalistic literature and research purposes
A data controller or data processor shall delete, erase, anonymise or pseudonymise personal data not necessary to be retained under sub-section (1)

sections.source_quote: A data controller or data processor shall retain personal data only as long as may be reasonably necessary to satisfy the purpose for which it is processed unless the retention is — (a) required or authorised by law; (b) reasonably necessary for a lawful purpose; (c) authorised or consented by the data subject; or (d) for historical, statistical, journalistic literature and research purposes.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data retention/deletion requirements

sections.heading: (1) A data subject may request a data controller or
sections.title: Right to rectification and erasure
sections.summary: Data subjects may request rectification of inaccurate, outdated, incomplete, or misleading personal data or erasure of irrelevant, excessive, or unlawfully obtained data. Data controllers must inform third parties when a data subject requests rectification or erasure. If data is required for evidence, processing must be restricted instead of erasure.
sections.dtia_summary: Rectification and erasure rights require controllers to notify third parties and restrict processing for evidence, impacting cross-border data transfers where data subjects may request corrections.

requirements.text: A data subject may request a data controller or data processor to rectify inaccurate, outdated, incomplete or misleading personal data
A data subject may request a data controller or data processor to erase or destroy irrelevant, excessive or unlawfully obtained personal data
Where a data controller has shared personal data with a third party, the data controller or processor shall inform third parties of the data subject's request for rectification or erasure
Where data is required for evidence, the data controller or processor shall restrict processing instead of erasing or rectifying

sections.source_quote: A data subject may request a data controller or data processor — (a) to rectify without undue delay personal data in its possession or under its control that is inaccurate, out-dated, incomplete or misleading; or (b) to erase or destroy without undue delay personal data that the data controller or data processor is no longer authorised to retain, irrelevant, excessive or obtained unlawfully.
sections.relevance_reason: Right to rectification and erasure directly addresses data subject rights under data protection law.

sections.heading: (1) Every data controller or data processor shall
sections.title: Data protection by design and default
sections.summary: Data controllers must implement technical and organizational measures to ensure data protection principles are effective and necessary safeguards are integrated into processing. Controllers must ensure only necessary data is processed by default, considering data amount, processing extent, storage period, accessibility, and processing costs. Controllers must identify risks, establish safeguards, pseudonymize or encrypt data, ensure availability, verify safeguards, and update them continuously.
sections.dtia_summary: Data protection by design requires technical and organizational measures for cross-border transfers, including pseudonymization and encryption to ensure data security during international transfers.

requirements.text: Data controllers must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to implement data protection principles effectively
Data controllers must integrate necessary safeguards into processing
Data controllers must ensure only necessary personal data is processed by default
Data controllers must consider data amount, processing extent, storage period, accessibility, and processing costs
Data controllers must identify reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to personal data
Data controllers must establish and maintain appropriate safeguards against identified risks
Data controllers must pseudonymize and encrypt personal data
Data controllers must ensure timely availability and access to personal data after a physical or technical incident
Data controllers must verify that safeguards are effectively implemented
Data controllers must ensure safeguards are continually updated in response to new risks or deficiencies

sections.source_quote: Every data controller or data processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures which are designed to implement the data protection principles in an effective manner; and to integrate necessary safeguards for that purpose into the processing.

sections.heading: (1) In determining the appropriate measures
sections.title: Organisational measures for cross-border data transfers
sections.summary: Data controllers must consider technological development, security measure costs, special risks, and data nature when determining organisational measures for cross-border transfers. Data controllers must select processors providing sufficient guarantees and enter written contracts ensuring processors act only on controller instructions. Processors acting outside controller instructions become controllers for that processing. All personnel must comply with security measures.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific organisational measures for cross-border transfers, including cost-benefit analysis and processor safeguards. Critical for assessing adequacy of transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: Data controller shall consider state of technological development
Data controller shall consider cost of implementing security measures
Data controller shall consider special risks in data processing
Data controller shall consider nature of data being processed
Data controller shall select processor providing sufficient guarantees for section 41(1)
Data controller and processor shall enter written contract ensuring processor acts only on controller instructions
Data processor acting outside controller instructions becomes controller for that processing
Data controller or processor shall take reasonable steps to ensure personnel comply with security measures

sections.source_quote: 42. (1) In determining the appropriate measures referred to in section 41, in particular, where the processing involves the transmission of data over an information and communication network, a data controller shall have regard to — (a) the state of technological development available; (b) the cost of implementing any of the security measures; (c) the special risks that exist in the processing of the data; and (d) the nature of the data being processed.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer security requirements

sections.heading: (1) Where personal data has been accessed or
sections.title: Breach notification requirements for personal data
sections.summary: Data controllers must notify the Data Commissioner within 72 hours of unauthorized access causing real harm risk. They must communicate breach details to data subjects within a reasonable period unless identity cannot be established. Processors must notify controllers within 48 hours. Notifications must include breach nature, remedial actions, and protective measures for data subjects. Controllers may delay communication for legal investigations. Breach records must include facts, effects, and remedial actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates specific breach notification timelines and content for cross-border data transfers, critical for assessing breach response adequacy in international contexts.

requirements.text: Data controller must notify Data Commissioner within 72 hours of unauthorized access causing real harm risk
Data controller must communicate breach details to data subject within reasonable period unless identity cannot be established
Data processor must notify data controller within 48 hours of breach
Breach notification must include nature of breach, remedial actions, and protective measures for data subjects
Data controller may delay communication for legal investigations
Breach records must include facts, effects, and remedial actions

sections.source_quote: 43. (1) Where personal data has been accessed or acquired by an unauthorised person, and there is a real risk of harm to the data subject whose personal data has been subjected to the unauthorised access, a data controller shall— (a) notify the Data Commissioner without delay, within seventy-two hours of becoming aware of such breach; and (b) subject to subsection (3), communicate to the data subject in writing within a reasonably practical period, unless the identity of the data subject cannot be established.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach notification core requirement

sections.heading: No category of sensitive personal data shall be
sections.title: Sensitive personal data processing requirements
sections.summary: No sensitive personal data may be processed unless section 25 applies. This section establishes that sensitive data processing requires specific legal grounds under section 25.

requirements.text: No category of sensitive personal data may be processed unless section 25 applies

sections.source_quote: 44. No category of sensitive personal data shall be processed unless section 25 applies to that processing.

sections.heading: Without prejudice to section 44, sensitive personal
sections.title: Permitted grounds for sensitive personal data processing
sections.summary: Sensitive personal data may be processed for legitimate activities by non-profits with specific safeguards, where data is publicly made available by the data subject, or for legal claims, rights exercise, or vital interests protection where the data subject is incapable of consent.

sections.source_quote: 45. Without prejudice to section 44, sensitive personal data of a data subject may be processed where — (a) the processing is carried out in the course of legitimate activities with appropriate safeguards by a foundation, association or any other not-for-profit body with a political, philosophical, religious or trade union aim and on condition that — (i) the processing relates solely to the members of the body or to persons who have regular contact with it in connection with its purposes; and (ii) the personal data is not disclosed outside that body without the consent of the data subject; (b) the processing relates to personal data which is manifestly made public by the data subject; or (c) processing is necessary for— (i) the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim; (ii) the purpose of carrying out the obligations and exercising specific rights of the controller or of the data subject; or (iii) protecting the vital interests of the data subject or another person where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent.

requirements.text: Health data may only be processed by healthcare providers
Health data may only be processed by persons under professional secrecy obligations

sections.heading: (1) The Data Commissioner may prescribe further
sections.title: Sensitive data classification and safeguards

sections.heading: A data controller or data processor may transfer
sections.title: Cross-border transfer of personal data: adequacy and safeguards
sections.summary: A data controller or processor may transfer personal data to another country only if they provide proof of appropriate security safeguards to the Data Commissioner. These safeguards must cover security and protection of personal data, and include jurisdictions with commensurate data protection laws. The transfer must also be necessary for specific purposes such as contractual obligations, legal claims, public interest, or vital interests of the data subject.
sections.dtia_summary: Cross-border transfers require proof of adequate security safeguards and commensurate data protection laws in the destination country. The transfer must be necessary for specific legal or contractual purposes.

Safeguards must include jurisdictions with commensurate data protection laws

Transfer must be necessary for conclusion or performance of contract in data subject's interest
Transfer must be necessary for matters of public interest
Transfer must be necessary for legal claims
Transfer must be necessary to protect vital interests of data subject or others when data subject is incapable of consent
Transfer must be necessary for compelling legitimate interests not overridden by data subjects' rights

sections.source_quote: A data controller or data processor may transfer personal data to another country only where — (a) the data controller or data processor has given proof to the Data Commissioner on the appropriate safeguards with respect to the security and protection of the personal data; (b) the data controller or data processor has given proof to the Data Commissioner of the appropriate safeguards with respect to the security and protection of personal data, and the appropriate safeguards including jurisdictions with commensurate data protection laws; (c) the transfer is necessary — (i) for the performance of a contract between the data subject and the data controller or data processor or implementation of pre-contractual measures taken at the data subject's request; (ii) for the conclusion or performance of a contract concluded in the interest of the data subject between the controller and another person; (iii) for any matter of public interest; (iv) for the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim; (v) in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of other persons, where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent; or (vi) for the purpose of compelling legitimate interests pursued by the data controller or data processor which are not overridden by the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subjects.

sections.heading: (1) The processing of sensitive personal data out of
sections.title: Sensitive data transfers: consent and safeguards
sections.summary: Sensitive personal data transfers out of Kenya require explicit consent from the data subject and proof of appropriate safeguards. The Data Commissioner can request demonstrations of security safeguards or compelling legitimate interests. The Commissioner may prohibit, suspend, or impose conditions on transfers to protect data subjects' rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Sensitive data transfers require explicit consent and proof of adequate safeguards. The Data Commissioner has authority to restrict transfers for data protection reasons.

requirements.text: Sensitive personal data transfers out of Kenya require explicit consent from data subject
Sensitive personal data transfers out of Kenya require confirmation of appropriate safeguards

sections.source_quote: The processing of sensitive personal data out of Kenya shall only be effected upon obtaining consent of a data subject and on obtaining confirmation of appropriate safeguards.
