sections.heading: The Cabinet Secretary may prescribe, based on
sections.title: Data residency for strategic interests
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary may require certain data processing activities to occur within Kenya based on strategic state interests or revenue protection. This applies to specific types of processing that must use servers or data centers located in Kenya.
sections.dtia_summary: Certain processing activities must be hosted within Kenya for strategic state interests or revenue protection, requiring data residency.

requirements.text: Cabinet Secretary may prescribe processing that must use servers or data centers in Kenya for strategic state interests or revenue protection

sections.source_quote: The Cabinet Secretary may prescribe, based on grounds of strategic interests of the state or protection of revenue, certain nature of processing that shall only be effected through a server or a data centre located in Kenya.
sections.relevance_reason: Data residency for strategic interests

sections.heading: (1) Nothing in this Part shall exempt any data
sections.title: General exemptions for data processing
sections.summary: Processing of personal data is exempt from this Act if it relates to purely personal/household activities, national security/public interest, or is required by law or court order. Data controllers must still comply with data protection principles for non-exempt processing.
sections.dtia_summary: Certain processing activities are exempt from data protection requirements, including personal/household use, national security, and legal obligations.

requirements.text: Processing of personal data is exempt if it relates to purely personal or household activity
Processing of personal data is exempt for national security or public interest
Processing of personal data is exempt if disclosure is required by law or court order
Data controllers must comply with data protection principles for non-exempt processing

sections.source_quote: The processing of personal data is exempt from the provisions of this Act if — (a) it relates to processing of personal data by an individual in the course of a purely personal or household activity; (b) if it is necessary for national security or public interest; or (c) disclosure is required by or under any written law or by an order of the court.
sections.relevance_reason: Exemptions directly impact data processing compliance

sections.heading: (1) The principles of processing personal data shall
sections.title: Journalism, literature and art exemptions
sections.summary: Processing for journalism, literature, and art is exempt from data protection requirements if it serves public interest, is in compliance with self-regulatory codes, and the Data Commissioner approves practical guidance. Data controllers must demonstrate public interest and compliance with ethical standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Journalism, literature, and art processing may be exempt from data protection requirements with specific conditions.

requirements.text: Processing for journalism, literature, and art is exempt if it serves public interest
Data controller must reasonably believe publication is in public interest
Data controller must reasonably believe compliance is incompatible with special purposes
Processing must comply with self-regulatory or issued codes of ethics

sections.source_quote: (1) The principles of processing personal data shall not apply where — (a) processing is undertaken by a person for the publication of a literary or artistic material; (b) data controller reasonably believes that publication would be in the public interest; and (c) data controller reasonably believes that, in all the circumstances, compliance with the provision is incompatible with the special purposes. (2) Subsection (1) (b) shall only apply where it can be demonstrated that the processing is in compliance with any self-regulatory or issued code of ethics in practice and relevant to the publication in question.
sections.relevance_reason: exemptions for journalism/literature/art

sections.heading: (1) The further processing of personal data shall
sections.title: Research data processing exemptions
sections.summary: Research data processing is exempt from data protection requirements if it is for historical, statistical, or research purposes and results are not identifiable. Data controllers must ensure data is not published in identifiable form and take measures to prevent misuse.
sections.dtia_summary: Research data processing may be exempt from data protection requirements if results are not identifiable and used solely for research purposes.

requirements.text: Research data must be used solely for historical, statistical, or research purposes
Data controller must ensure further processing does not publish data in identifiable form
Data controller must take measures to prevent records from being used for other purposes
Research data is exempt if results are not made available in identifiable form

sections.source_quote: The further processing of personal data shall be compatible with the purpose of collection if the data is used for historical, statistical or research purposes and the data controller or data processor shall ensure that the further processing is carried out solely for such purposes and will not be published in an identifiable form.
sections.relevance_reason: Research data exemptions directly impact data processing compliance

sections.heading: (1) The Data Commissioner may issue a data
sections.title: Data sharing code for government data exchange
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may issue a data sharing code that includes practical guidance for sharing personal data in compliance with data protection laws and additional guidance to promote good practices. This code must specify lawful exchange mechanisms between government departments and public sector agencies. The code serves as a framework for ensuring consistent and lawful data sharing across public institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for standardized data sharing practices between government entities, which may impact cross-border data transfers if public sector agencies share data internationally.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may issue a data sharing code containing practical guidance for sharing personal data in accordance with data protection legislation
The data sharing code must specify lawful exchange of personal data between government departments or public sector agencies

sections.source_quote: 55. (1) The Data Commissioner may issue a data sharing code which shall contain — (a) practical guidance in relation to the sharing of personal data in accordance with the requirements of the data protection legislation; and (b) such other guidance as the Commissioner considers appropriate to promote good practice in the sharing of personal data.
sections.relevance_reason: Government data sharing compliance framework

sections.heading: (1) A data subject who is aggrieved by a decision
sections.title: Complaint procedures for data subjects
sections.summary: Data subjects can lodge complaints with the Data Commissioner if they are aggrieved by decisions made under the Act. Complaints must be made orally or in writing, with oral complaints being recorded in writing. Complaints must include prescribed particulars and be investigated within ninety days. The Data Commissioner has authority to investigate and conclude complaints within this timeframe.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines complaint procedures that may be relevant for data transfers involving government entities, ensuring accountability in data handling practices.

requirements.text: A data subject aggrieved by a decision under this Act may lodge a complaint with the Data Commissioner
Complaints must be made orally or in writing
Oral complaints must be recorded in writing by the Data Commissioner
Complaints must contain such particulars as the Data Commissioner prescribes
Complaints must be investigated and concluded within ninety days

sections.source_quote: 56. (1) A data subject who is aggrieved by a decision of any person under this Act may lodge a complaint with the Data Commissioner in accordance with this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Data subject complaint procedures

sections.heading: (1) The Data Commissioner may, for the purpose
sections.title: Compliance with complaint investigations
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may order individuals to attend examinations, produce documents, or provide sworn statements during complaint investigations. The Data Commissioner may require access to electronic data for investigations. Failure to comply with these orders constitutes an offense.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides investigative powers for data compliance checks that may impact data handling practices during cross-border transfers involving government entities.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may order a person to attend for oral examination in relation to a complaint
The Data Commissioner may order a person to produce documents, records, or articles relevant to the investigation
The Data Commissioner may require a person to furnish a written statement under oath or affirmation
The Data Commissioner may require electronic data to be produced in a visible and legible form
Failure to comply with investigation orders without reasonable excuse constitutes an offense

sections.source_quote: 57. (1) The Data Commissioner may, for the purpose of the investigation of a complaint, order any person to — (a) attend at a specified time and place for the purpose of being examined orally in relation to the complaint; (b) produce such book, document, record or article as may be required with respect to any matter relevant to the investigation, which the person is not prevented by any other enactment from disclosing; or (c) furnish a statement in writing made under oath or on affirmation setting out all information which may be required under the notice.
sections.relevance_reason: Compliance with complaint investigations

sections.heading: (1) Where the Data Commissioner is satisfied that
sections.title: Enforcement notices for non-compliance
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may issue enforcement notices requiring persons to take corrective actions for non-compliance with the Act. These notices must specify the violated provision, required remedial steps, a minimum twenty-one-day implementation period, and appeal rights. Failure to comply with enforcement notices is an offense punishable by fines up to five million shillings or imprisonment for two years.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes enforcement mechanisms that may be triggered during data transfers involving government entities, requiring corrective actions for non-compliance.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may serve an enforcement notice requiring a person to take steps to comply with the Act
An enforcement notice must specify the provision of the Act that has been contravened
An enforcement notice must specify measures to remedy or eliminate contraventions
An enforcement notice must specify a minimum twenty-one-day period for implementation
An enforcement notice must state any right of appeal
Failure to comply with an enforcement notice without reasonable excuse constitutes an offense

sections.source_quote: 58. (1) Where the Data Commissioner is satisfied that a person has failed, or is failing, to comply with any provision of this Act, the Data Commissioner may serve an enforcement notice on that person requiring that person to take such steps and within such period as may be specified in the notice.
sections.relevance_reason: enforcement notices for non-compliance

sections.heading: For the purpose of gathering information or for
sections.title: Data Commissioner's assistance requests
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may seek assistance from persons or authorities to gather information or conduct investigations under the Act. This provision enables the Data Commissioner to obtain necessary support for fulfilling their functions related to data protection.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows the Data Commissioner to access external resources for investigations, which may impact data handling practices during cross-border transfers involving government entities.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may seek assistance from persons or authorities for gathering information or investigations

sections.source_quote: 59. For the purpose of gathering information or for any investigation under this Act, the Data Commissioner may seek the assistance of such person or authority as they deem fit and as is reasonably necessary to assist the Data Commissioner in the discharge of their functions.
sections.relevance_reason: Data Commissioner's powers for investigations

sections.heading: The Data Commissioner, upon obtaining a warrant
sections.title: Search powers for Data Commissioner
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may enter and search premises with a court warrant to discharge functions or exercise powers under the Act. This provision grants the Data Commissioner authority to conduct physical searches for data-related purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides search powers that may be relevant for data transfers involving government entities, ensuring compliance with data protection requirements.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may enter and search any premises with a court warrant for the purpose of discharging functions under this Act

sections.source_quote: 60. The Data Commissioner, upon obtaining a warrant from a Court, may enter and search any premises for the purpose of discharging any function or exercising any power under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Search powers for Data Commissioner

sections.heading: A person who, in relation to the exercise of a
sections.title: Obstruction of Data Commissioner's powers
sections.summary: A person commits an offence if they obstruct the Data Commissioner in exercising powers under section 9, fail to provide requested assistance, refuse entry to premises, or give false/misleading information. The offence carries a fine of up to five million shillings or imprisonment for two years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes penalties for non-compliance with Data Commissioner's investigations, relevant for DTIAs involving obstruction of data protection authorities.

requirements.text: Obstructs or impedes the Data Commissioner in the exercise of their powers
Fails to provide assistance or information requested by the Data Commissioner
Refuses to allow the Data Commissioner to enter any premises or take any person
Gives to the Data Commissioner any information which is false or misleading in any material aspect

sections.source_quote: A person who, in relation to the exercise of a power conferred by section 9— (a) obstructs or impedes the Data Commissioner in the exercise of their powers; (b) fails to provide assistance or information requested by the Data Commissioner; (c) refuses to allow the Data Commissioner to enter any premises or to take any person with them in the exercise of their functions; (d) gives to the Data Commissioner any information which is false or misleading in any material aspect, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Obstruction of Data Commissioner's powers directly impacts data compliance

sections.heading: (1) If the Data Commissioner is satisfied that a
sections.title: Penalty notices for data protection breaches
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may issue penalty notices requiring payment to the Office for failures described in section 58. The penalty amount is determined based on factors including the nature of the failure, intent, mitigation efforts, responsibility, prior failures, cooperation, data categories affected, notification history, compliance with previous notices, adherence to codes of conduct, and financial impacts.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details penalty calculation criteria for data breaches, critical for DTIAs assessing financial penalties and enforcement risks.

requirements.text: If the Data Commissioner is satisfied that a person has failed or is failing as described in section 58, the Data Commissioner may issue a penalty notice requiring the person to pay to the Office of the Data Commissioner an amount specified in the notice
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider the nature, gravity and duration of the failure
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider the intentional or negligent character of the failure
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider any action taken by the data controller or data processor to mitigate damage or distress
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider the degree of responsibility of the data controller or data processor
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider any relevant previous failures by the data controller or data processor
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider the degree of co-operation with the Data Commissioner to remedy the failure
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider the categories of personal data affected by the failure
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider the manner in which the infringement became known to the Data Commissioner

In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider the extent to which the data controller or data processor has complied with previous enforcement notices or penalty notices
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider adherence to approved codes of conduct or certification mechanisms
In deciding whether to give a penalty notice and determining the amount, the Data Commissioner shall consider any other aggravating or mitigating factor applicable to the case

sections.source_quote: If the Data Commissioner is satisfied that a person has failed or is failing as described in section 58, the Data Commissioner may issue a penalty notice requiring the person to pay to the Office of the Data Commissioner an amount specified in the notice.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalty notices for breach compliance

sections.heading: In relation to an infringement of a provision of this
sections.title: Maximum penalty amounts for data breaches
sections.summary: The maximum penalty for a data breach is five million shillings or one percent of the data controller's annual turnover from the previous financial year, whichever is lower. This cap ensures penalties are proportionate to the entity's size.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets the upper limit for penalties, critical for DTIAs to assess financial risks and ensure penalties are proportionate.

requirements.text: The maximum amount of the penalty that may be imposed by the Data Commissioner in a penalty notice is up to five million shillings
In the case of an undertaking, the maximum penalty is up to one per centum of its annual turnover of the preceding financial year

sections.source_quote: In relation to an infringement of a provision of this Act, the maximum amount of the penalty that may be imposed by the Data Commissioner in a penalty notice is up to five million shillings, or in the case of an undertaking, up to one per centum of its annual turnover of the preceding financial year, whichever is lower.
sections.relevance_reason: core penalty for data breaches

sections.heading: (1) A person who suffers damage by reason of a
sections.title: Compensation for data breach victims
sections.summary: A data controller or processor liable for damage caused by a breach must compensate victims. The data controller is liable for all damage, while the processor is liable only if they failed to comply with specific obligations or acted contrary to the controller's lawful instructions. Compensation includes financial loss and non-financial harm like distress.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines liability for data breaches and compensation, essential for DTIAs assessing financial and reputational impacts on data transfers.

requirements.text: A person who suffers damage by reason of a contravention of a requirement of this Act is entitled to compensation for that damage from the data controller or the data processor
A data controller involved in processing of personal data is liable for any damage caused by the processing

A data processor involved in processing of personal data is liable for damage caused by the processing only if the processor has not complied with an obligation under the Act specifically directed at data processors
A data processor involved in processing of personal data is liable for damage caused by the processing only if the processor has acted outside or contrary to the data controller's lawful instructions
A data controller or data processor is not liable in the manner specified in subsection (2) if they prove they are not responsible for the event causing damage
In this section, 'damage' includes financial loss and damage not involving financial loss, including distress

sections.source_quote: A person who suffers damage by reason of a contravention of a requirement of this Act is entitled to compensation for that damage from the data controller or the data processor.
sections.relevance_reason: Compensation for breach victims directly addresses data breach liability

sections.heading: The Data Commissioner may apply to a court for a
sections.title: Preservation orders for vulnerable data
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner can request a court to preserve personal data, including traffic data, if there's reasonable grounds that it's vulnerable to loss or modification. This ensures critical data remains intact during investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for data preservation during investigations, relevant for DTIAs involving data storage and handling during breaches.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may apply to a court for a preservation order for the expeditious preservation of personal data including traffic data
Where there is reasonable ground to believe that the data is vulnerable to loss or modification

sections.source_quote: The Data Commissioner may apply to a court for a preservation order for the expeditious preservation of personal data including traffic data, where there is reasonable ground to believe that the data is vulnerable to loss or modification.
sections.relevance_reason: Preservation orders for vulnerable data

sections.heading: The funds and assets of the Office shall consist
sections.title: Funds for the Data Protection Office
sections.summary: The Data Protection Office's funds consist of allocations from the National Assembly, grants, gifts, donations, and funds accrued through its functions under the Act. This ensures the Office has sufficient resources for enforcement activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is not directly relevant to data transfers but ensures the Data Commissioner has adequate resources for enforcement.
sections.source_quote: The funds and assets of the Office shall consist of— (a) monies allocated by the National Assembly for purposes of the Office; (b) any grants, gifts, donations or other endowments given to the Office; and (c) such funds as may vest in or accrue to the Office in the performance of its functions under this Act or any other written law.
sections.relevance_reason: funds allocation mechanics

sections.heading: (1) At least three months before the
sections.title: Annual financial estimates preparation for Office
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner must prepare annual financial estimates at least three months before each financial year starts. These estimates cover all expected expenditures including staff salaries, retirement benefits, equipment maintenance, training, reserve funds, and other office-related costs. The estimates must be submitted to the Cabinet Secretary for tabling in the National Assembly.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner shall cause to be prepared estimates of the revenue and expenditure of the Office for each financial year
The annual estimates shall make provision for all estimated expenditure of the Office for the financial year
The annual estimates shall provide for payment of salaries, allowances and other charges for staff
The annual estimates shall provide for payment of pensions, gratuities and other retirement benefits
The annual estimates shall cover acquisition, maintenance, repair and replacement of equipment and movable property
The annual estimates shall fund training, research and development activities
The annual estimates shall create reserve funds for future or contingent liabilities
The annual estimates shall cover any other expenditure for purposes of this Act
The annual estimates shall be submitted to the Cabinet Secretary for tabling in the National Assembly

sections.source_quote: 68. (1) At least three months before the commencement of each financial year, the Data Commissioner shall cause to be prepared estimates of the revenue and expenditure of the Office for that year.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial administration, not data compliance

sections.heading: The annual accounts of the Office shall be
sections.title: Annual accounts preparation and audit requirements
sections.summary: The Office must prepare, audit and report its annual accounts following constitutional provisions, the Public Finance Management Act, 2012, or other applicable audit laws. This includes compliance with specific financial reporting standards for public entities to ensure transparency and accountability in financial management.

requirements.text: The annual accounts of the Office shall be prepared, audited and reported in accordance with Articles 226 and 229 of the Constitution
The annual accounts of the Office shall be prepared, audited and reported in accordance with the Public Finance Management Act, 2012
The annual accounts of the Office shall be prepared, audited and reported in accordance with any other law relating to audit of public entities

sections.source_quote: 69. The annual accounts of the Office shall be prepared, audited and reported in accordance with the provisions of Articles 226 and 229 of the Constitution, the Public Finance Management Act, 2012, or any other law relating to audit of public entities.

sections.heading: (1) The Data Commissioner shall, within three
sections.title: Annual operational reports submission requirements
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner must submit an annual report within three months after each financial year ends. This report includes financial statements, activity descriptions, statistical information, impact assessments of mandates, impediments to the Act's objectives, and other necessary information related to the Office's functions.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner shall prepare and submit to the Cabinet Secretary a report of the operations of the Office for the immediately preceding year
The Cabinet Secretary shall submit the annual report before the National Assembly within three months of receipt of the report
The annual report shall contain financial statements and description of activities of the Office
The annual report shall contain statistical information as the Data Commissioner considers appropriate
The annual report shall contain impact of exercise of Data Commissioner's mandate or function
The annual report shall contain impediments to achievements of the object and purpose of this Act
The annual report shall contain any other information relating to functions that the Data Commissioner considers necessary

sections.source_quote: 70. (1) The Data Commissioner shall, within three months after the end of each financial year, prepare and submit to the Cabinet Secretary a report of the operations of the Office for the immediately preceding year.

sections.heading: The Cabinet Secretary may, make regulations
sections.title: Regulations for implementing Data Protection Act
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary may make regulations to implement this Act and prescribe necessary matters including data controller/processor requirements, certification programs, notice/registration contents, data subject information provisions, fee levies, data protection safeguards, data center processing in Kenya, and codes of practice. These regulations must follow statutory instruments and constitutional principles.

requirements.text: The Cabinet Secretary may make regulations generally for giving effect to this Act
Regulations may provide for requirements imposed on data controllers or data processors when processing personal data
Regulations may provide for mechanisms of conducting certification programs
Regulations may specify contents for notices or registrations by data controllers or data processors
Regulations may specify information to be provided to data subjects and how such information shall be provided
Regulations may provide for levying fees and taking charges
Regulations may provide for measures to safeguard data subjects' rights, freedoms and legitimate interests
Regulations may provide for processing of data through data servers or data centres in Kenya
Regulations may issue and approve codes of practice and guidelines

sections.source_quote: 71. The Cabinet Secretary may, make regulations generally for giving effect to this Act, and for prescribing anything required or necessary to be prescribed by or under this Act.

sections.heading: (1) A data controller who, without lawful excuse,
sections.title: Unlawful disclosure of personal data offences
sections.summary: Disclosing personal data in a manner incompatible with the purpose of collection constitutes an offence for data controllers without lawful excuse. Data processors who disclose data without data controller's prior authority also commit offences. Unauthorized access to personal data or disclosure to third parties without authorization is prohibited, except for employees/agents acting within their mandate.

requirements.text: A data controller who discloses personal data in any manner incompatible with the purpose for which such data was collected commits an offence
A data processor who discloses personal data without prior authority of the data controller commits an offence
A person who obtains access to personal data without prior authority of the data controller or data processor commits an offence
A person who discloses personal data to third party commits an offence
Subsection (3) does not apply to employees or agents of data controllers or processors acting within the scope of their mandate
A person who offers to sell personal data obtained in breach of subsection (1) commits an offence
An advertisement indicating personal data is or may be for sale constitutes an offer to sell the personal data

sections.source_quote: 72. (1) A data controller who, without lawful excuse, discloses personal data in any manner that is incompatible with the purpose for which such data has been collected commits an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Directly addresses unlawful data disclosure and offences

sections.heading: (1) A person who commits an offence under this
sections.title: Penalties for data protection offences
sections.summary: Persons who commit offences under this Act without specific penalties or contravene the Act may be liable to a fine of up to three million shillings or imprisonment of up to ten years, or both. Courts may also order forfeiture of equipment used in offences or prohibit continuing contraventions.

requirements.text: A person who commits an offence under this Act for which no specific penalty is provided or contravenes this Act shall be liable to a fine not exceeding three million shillings
A person who commits an offence under this Act for which no specific penalty is provided or contravenes this Act shall be liable to imprisonment not exceeding ten years
A person who commits an offence under this Act for which no specific penalty is provided or contravenes this Act may be liable to both a fine and imprisonment
Courts may order forfeiture of equipment or articles used in commission of an offence
Courts may order or prohibit acts to stop continuing contraventions

sections.source_quote: 73. (1) A person who commits an offence under this Act for which no specific penalty is provided or who otherwise contravenes this Act shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding three million shillings or to an imprisonment term not exceeding ten years, or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for data breaches

sections.heading: (1) The Data Commissioner may, for the purpose
sections.title: Data Commissioner's authority to issue guidelines and certification standards
sections.summary: The Data Commissioner may issue guidelines or codes of practice for data controllers, processors, and data protection officers. The Commissioner can also establish certification standards and seals to encourage compliance with the Data Protection Act. Additionally, the Commissioner may require third parties to certify or adhere to codes of practice. The Commissioner may develop sector-specific guidelines in consultation with stakeholders in areas like health, financial services, education, and social protection. Certification does not alter the data controller or processor's responsibility for compliance with the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section empowers the Data Commissioner to create certification standards and sector-specific guidelines that may influence cross-border data processing requirements and third-party data handling practices.

requirements.text: The Data Commissioner may issue guidelines or codes of practice for data controllers, data processors and data protection officers
The Data Commissioner may offer data protection certification standards and data protection seals to encourage compliance with this Act
The Data Commissioner may require certification or adherence to code of practice by a third party

The Data Commissioner may develop sector specific guidelines in consultation with relevant stakeholders in areas such as health, financial services, education, social protection and any other area as the Data Commissioner may determine
Certification issued under this section shall not alter the responsibility of the data controller or data processor for compliance with this Act

sections.source_quote: 74. (1) The Data Commissioner may, for the purpose of this Act — (a) issue guidelines or codes of practice for the data controllers, data processors and data protection officers; (b) offer data protection certification standards and data protection seals and marks in order to encourage compliance of processing operations with this Act; (c) require certification or adherence to code of practice by a third party; (d) develop sector specific guidelines in consultation with relevant stakeholders in areas such as health, financial services, education, social protection and any other area as the Data Commissioner may determine. (2) A certification issued under this section shall not alter the responsibility of the data controller or data processor for compliance with this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Data Commissioner's certification standards for compliance

sections.heading: The laws specified under the Second Schedule are
sections.title: Consequential amendments to laws for data protection compliance
sections.summary: This section amends multiple laws to incorporate data protection principles from the Data Protection Act. It requires various laws to align their processing of personal data with the Data Protection Act's principles, including financial services, employment, education, and other sectors. Specific amendments include adding new sections to ensure data protection principles apply to capital markets, electoral commissions, and other institutions. These amendments mandate that personal data be handled in accordance with data protection principles across different regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that multiple laws be amended to align with data protection principles, which may impact cross-border data transfers and processing by third parties in financial and other regulated sectors.

requirements.text: The Register shall be maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection set out in the Data Protection Act
Ensure processing of personal data in the operations of capital markets is in accordance with principles set out under the Data Protection Act

The principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply to the collection and processing of personal data by the Authority or any person authorized by the Authority
Mechanisms of protecting personal data of the data subjects in compliance with the Data Protection Act
The principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply to the processing of personal data of voters
The Commission shall ensure the management of personal data is in accordance with the principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act
Align Regulations on the collection and processing of information which consists of personal data with the Data Protection Act
Where an employer maintains such a register, the register shall be maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act
Personal data of individuals obtained under this Act shall be held and maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection set out in the Data Protection Act
The Board shall deal with any relevant personal data collected and so held in the register according to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
Any information containing personal data presented to the Commission shall be handled in accordance with data protection principles set out in the Data Protection Act
A record of depositors required by an issuer under sub-section (1) shall be issued and maintained in accordance with the principles of data protection set out in the Data Protection Act
The disclosure of information under this Act shall be done according to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
The sharing of information by the Centre shall be with adherence to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
The information collected on natural persons under this section shall be dealt according to the data principles set out in the Data Protection Act
Ensure necessary steps are taken to secure the integrity of personal data through technical and organizational measures
The principles of personal data protection set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply with necessary modifications to the processing and handling of bankrupt's personal data

sections.source_quote: 75. The laws specified under the Second Schedule are amended in the manner specified. The Data Protection Act 2019: CONSEQUENTIAL AMENDMENTS: Insert the following new section immediately after section 13B — 13C. The principles of personal data protection as set out in the Data Protection Act shall apply to the collection and processing of personal data by the Authority or any person authorized by the Authority.
